Baseline emissions of methane from the SWDS are determined as follows, based on the amount 
of methane that is captured under the project activity and the amount that would be captured 
and destroyed in the baseline (such as due to regulations). In addition, the effect of methane 
oxidation that is present in the baseline and absent in the project is taken into accou nt: 
𝐵𝐸𝐶𝐻4 = ((1 − 𝑂𝑋𝑡𝑜𝑝_𝑙𝑎𝑦𝑒𝑟) × 𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝑃𝑗,𝑦 − 𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝐵𝐿,𝑦) × 𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 Equation (2)

in the baseline (dimensionless)

𝐹 𝐶𝐻4,𝐸𝐿,𝑦 is determined using the “Tool to determine the mass flow of a greenhouse gas in a 
gaseous stream”. The following requirements apply:  
(e) As per the gaseous stream tool, if the LFG is used for multiple purposes (e.g. flaring or 
energy generation), and all methane destruction devices are verified to be operational (e.g. by 
means of flame detectors records, energy generated), a single flow  meter may be used to

Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
164 
record the flow into multiple destruction devices. The destruction efficiency of the least 
efficient among the destruction devices shall be used as the destruction efficiency for all 
destruction devices monitored by this flow meter. If there are any period s for which one or more 
destruction devices are not operational, paragraph 5 (a) and (b) of the Appendix of the "Tool to 
determine the mass flow of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream" tool shall be followed;  
(f) CH4 is the greenhouse gas for which the mass flow should be determined;  
(g) The simplification offered for calculating the molecular mass of the gaseous stream is valid 
(equations (3) or (17) in the tool);  
(h) The mass flow should be calculated on an hourly basis for each hour h in year y;  
(i) The mass flow calculated for hour h is 0 if the equipment is not working in hour h (Opj,h=not 
working), the hourly values are then summed to a yearly unit basis.  
Amount of the methane used for power generation ( 𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝐸𝐿,𝑦) The “Tool to determine the mass 
of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream” provides following 6 options for measuring mass 
flow of a greenhouse gas i in a gaseous stream Table.  
Option Flow gaseous stream Volumetric Fraction 
A Volume flow – dry basis Dry or wet basis 
B Volume flow – wet basis Dry basis 
C Volume flow – wet basis Wet basis 
D Mass flow – dry basis Dry or wet basis  
E Mass flow – wet basis Dry basis 
F Mass flow – wet basis Wet basis 
 
Under project activity, Biogas flow measurements are converted Nm 3 by the flow meter79 
(corrected volume flow, page 9 of document) which use data from connected to the 
temperature and pressure sensors. Thus, measured values are automatically converted to 
normal conditions (0 °C and 101,325 Pa). According to methane density document of CDM 80, 
0.716 kg/m3 is the density at normal conditions. Further applying Option A from TOOL08 would 
result to overcounting baseline emissions.  
Thus biogas density value of 0.716 kg/m 3 (normal conditions) is taken from TOOL08 “Tool to 
determine the mass of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream”.

Baseline emissions associated with electricity generation (BE EC,y) 
The baseline emissions associated with electricity generation in year y (BE EC,y) shall be 
calculated using the "Methodological tool: Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions from 
electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity generation". When applying the tool:  
(a) The electricity sources k in the tool correspond to the sources of electricity generated 
identified in the selection of the most plausible baseline scenario; and

Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
166 
(b) ECBL,k,y in the tool is equivalent to the net amount of electricity generated using LFG in year y 
(EGPJ,y). 
Taking into account the approach provided by the tool, baseline emissions are then calculated 
using the generic approach based on the quantity of electricity dispatched into the National 
Grid, an emission factor for electricity generation and a factor to account for transmission 
losses, as follows: 
𝐵𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 = 𝛴𝐸𝐶𝐵𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 × 𝐸𝐹𝐸𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 × (1 + 𝑇𝐷𝐿𝑘,𝑦) Equation (2) 
 
Where; 
𝐸𝐶𝐵𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 = Net amount of electricity generated using LFG in year y (MWh/yr)  
𝐸𝐹𝐸𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 = Emission factor for electricity generation for source k in year y (t CO 2/MWh) 
𝑇𝐷𝐿𝑘,𝑦 = Average technical transmission and distribution losses for providing electricity to source 
k in year y 
k = Sources of electricity generated in the baseline  
The latest Emission Factor is given from Turkish National Electricity Network calculated annually 
and for the year 2020 the combined emission factor is 0 .5410 tCO2/MWh and. The average 
technical transmission and distribution losses factor is 9.9703 for year 2020, 9.630 0% for year 
2021, 9.1018% for year 2022 and 9.2181% for year 2023. 
BEEC,y = 1,739.805 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.099703) = 1,035 tCO2e (year 2020, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 21,201.337 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.096300) = 12,574 tCO2e (year 2021, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 40,625.781 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.091018) = 23,978 tCO2e (year 2022, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 4,142.851 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.092181) = 2,447 tCO2e (year 2023, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 1,035 + 12,574 + 23,978 + 2,447 = 40,034

According to appendix of TOOL08, data substitution is possible to calculate methane mass flow.

Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
172 
If missing data are encountered in the course of determining the methane mass flow, it  may be 
substituted with conservative data sets (see below) from specific periods.  However, data 
substitution shall only be applied to either the methane concentration or  the biogas volumetric  
flow readings, but not to both simultaneously. If data is missing for  both parameters during a 
given period of time, no data substitution shall be allowed for  that period. 
Substitution as outlined in table below may be undertaken only if the following  conditions are 
met: 
(a) For methane concentration, biogas flow rates during the period where data gap occurred (data 
gap period) shall be consistent with normal operation (i.e. the  average flow rates during the gap 
period shall not deviate from the average flow rates of the period taken for data substitution (data 
substitution period) by more than +/ - 20%); and 
(b) For biogas flow rate, methane concentration during the data gap period shall be  consistent 
with the methane concentration observed during normal operations (i.e.  the average methane 
concentration during the data gap period shall not deviate  from the average methane 
concentration of the data substitution period by more  than +/- 20%); and 
(c) Project participants shall demonstrate that the methane is being destroyed during  the period 
of the data gap. If corroborating parameters fail to demonstrate any of  these requirements, no 
substitution shall be allowed.  
 
By following outlined rules, a data substitution procedure is utilized to realize achieved GHG 
emissions.  
Duration of Missing Data  Data Substitution procedure  
Less than six hours  Use the weighted average of the four hours 
period immediately before and four hours 
period immediately after the outage  
Six to 24 hours Use the upper bound or lower bound of 95% 
confidence interval of the data spanning 24 
hours prior to and 24 hours after the outage, 
whichever results in more conservative 
estimate of emission reductions  
One to seven days Use the upper bound or lower bound of 95% 
confidence 
Greater than one week interval of the data spanning 72 hours prior to 
and 72 hours

capacity of liquid digestate (fraction)

𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (28 tCO2 / tCH4) 
 
According to TOOL14, Fww,CH4,default is selected based on the technology of anerobic digesters. 
Under project activity, plug flow reactors under thermophilic conditions are utilized. Due to high 
COD removal efficiency of such reactors, minimum default value of "0.05" is selected.

Justification for the difference  
137,692 
152,852 11.010% 1. Currently, landfill gas is being generated from two 
lots. Lot-1 is full but remains active, while waste 
disposal continues in Lot -2. Waste deposition in 
Lot-1 began in 2013. Although the project has 
been a sanitary landfill from the start, the current 
site management is more efficient, with a greater 
focus on landfill gas collection through improved 
cover and gas collection systems, leading to 
better landfill gas collection. The historical waste 
projection is based on a population model, which 
inherently carries uncertainty regarding both the 
disposed waste volume and its composition. Ex -
ante baseline emissions from the SWDS are 
estimated at 94 ,739 tCO₂e, while ex-post 
emission reduction for the same category 
amounts to 135,140 tCO ₂e. While this difference 
may seem significant, it can be attributed to 
inherent uncertainties in the projection model  
made with best available data . Also as stated 
below, biomethanization plant’s commissioning is 
delayed by eight months and during this time  all 
MSW is disposed to landfill.  
2. "According to the applied methodology ACM0022 
Version 03.0, Paragraph 33, ‘Baseline methane 
emissions from the SWDS are determined using 
“TOOL04: Emissions from solid waste disposal 
sites.”’ The TOOL04 model estimates the amount 
of methane that would have been generated 
under baseline conditions. This represen ts a 
conservative approach, as baseline emission 
credit generation accelerates over time. The 
introduction of the biomethanization facility was 
delayed until mid -2021, and waste records for 
July 2021 were unavailable. As a result, only 
10,363 tCO ₂e (rounded down) out of the 
expected 40,362 tCO₂e was achieved during the 
first crediting period. Due to this delay, organic 
waste was disposed of in the landfill for eight 
months after electricity generation began."  
3. Net electricity generation is achieved as 
67,709.774 MWh while estimation was 
73,637.783 MWh. The difference of 8.050% is 
within the acceptable margin of error.   
4. During the two -year pandemic period (2019 –
2021), global stay -at-home measures led to an 
increase in solid waste with higher food content

Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
185 
being disposed of. This resulted in enhanced gas 
generation from landfills prior to the introduction 
of the biomethanization facility .83

1 PROJECT DETAILS 
1.1 Summary Description of the Project 
Bergama integrated solid waste management facility and electricity generation facility  is a waste 
handling and disposal project where Municipal Solid Wastes (MSW) from several districts of Izmir 
province is disposed to Bergama Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility , mechanically 
separated and utilized for power generation. Upon the acceptance of mixed MSW, waste streams 
are separated based on type at the Mechanical Separation Facility and, the biodegradable wastes 
are sent to  biomethanization Facility . The remaining fractions are sent to the existing sanitary 
landfill and recovered recyclable wastes is sent to licensed companies. The biogas generated 
from the biomethanization facility, and the generated LFG is converted into electrical energy by 
gas engines then supplied to the Türkiye’s national grid thus generates revenue. Liquid and 
fermented products are found polluted by project owner due to poor waste separation at source  
public practice therefore cannot be utilized as intended. Solid fraction is sent to landfill and liquid 
fraction is treated as leachate. Due to project activity, the waste is stabilized, the amount of waste 
to be stored is minimized, electricity  generation is  utilized, and the negative environmental 
effects of wastes are eliminated. RDF production is also planned and to be implemented after 
January 2024. It will be used for electricity generation in  Cine Biomass Power Plant and Ezine 
Biomass Power Plant which are operated by subsidiary company of İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik 
Üretim A.Ş. 
Electricity generation capacity (licence capacity) of project is 9.898 MWe (10.157 MWm) and 
project, landfill are located in Izmir Province, Bergama District, Kaşıkçı Neighborhood . Project is 
owned and operated by İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik Üretim A.Ş.   
Prior to project implementation, MSW was disposed of to sanitary landfill where generated LFG 
was vented to atmosphere. Project activity contributed to following goals  
• Reduction in fossil fuel use (imported or local) by using renewable energy resources  
• Displacement of ecologically unsound and climate unfriendly power generation  
• Extension of nationally sourced power generation  
• Improvement of environmental conditions (GHG and odour) and safety in the landfill area  
• Reduce the greenhouse gas emissions  from being emitted directly to the atmosphere from 
waste at the Bergama SWDS that would be otherwise left to be decomposed. , 
Over the projected 7 -year crediting period, the project is estimated to achieve a net emission 
reduction of 1,378,074 tCO2e (metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent) and 196,867 tCO2e as 
annual average. Estimated gross electricity generation is expected to reach full capacity, at year 
2024, which is 74,235 MWh/year and cumulative gross electricity generation  estimation for the

commissioning of gas engines. Between equipment tests and gas engine, flare equipment was 
not operational.

1.11 Description of the Project Activity 
The project activity includes waste reception area, mechanical separation unit, sanitary landfill, 
biomethanization facility (anaerobic reactors)  and LFG to power plant. 
Mixed municipal wastes collected from the project area will be brought to the waste reception 
unit of the Facility and after all of them enter the Mechanical Separation Facility, they will be 
separated as biodegradable waste, residual waste, and waste suitable for recycling.  
Majority of the biodegradable waste are sent to the Biomethanization Facility, and residual 
biodegradable wastes that cannot be recovered  are sent to the Sanitary Landfill Area along with 
inert fractions. Project is within the scope of the Temporary Article 1 of the Regulation on Regular 
Storage of Wastes, which was published in the Official Gazette dated 26 -March-2010 and 
numbered 27533.  
Portion of the recycled wastes that are suitable for recovery is sent to licensed disposal facilities 
and contribute to the circular economy. Remaining waste  fraction is sent to the Landfill for 
disposal.

The capacity of the Mechanical Separation Facility to be established within the scope of the 
project has been determined as 435 tons/day  initially, taking into account the total waste 
generation amount to be generated according to the population of Bergama and Dikili district in 
the target year 2035. The units to be found in the system 3 are given below. It will be a fully 
automatic system without manual separation.

• Bale Press

machine are stored) 
• Product Warehouse (Biodegradable wastes and residual wastes will be collected in closed 
trailers that will be constantly present)  
The biodegradable fraction of MSW separated  from the Mechanical Separation Facility is utilized 
by Biomethanization Facility, where 27.30%  is converted to  solid fermented product, 36.35%  is 
converted to liquid fermented product and 36.35% is decomposition loss  (biogas). 
A part of the liquid fermented product formed in the Biomethanization Facility is recycled back to 
the system  and, the remaining portion is se nt to the balancing pool . In order to meet the 
conditions for the discharge of water into the sewer system in accordance with the provisions of 
the Water Pollution Control Regulation and the IZSU Wastewater Discharge into the Sewage 
Network, liquid fermented products along with leachate is sent to leachate treatment facility .  
Approximately 75% of the liquid fermented product is recycled back to the system. The remaining 
25% is transferred to the treatment plant to be established within the scope of the project.  
The solid fermented product formed in the Biomethanization Facility is directed to the landfill due 
to fact it doesn’t satisfy criteria to be used as fertilizer. Non-standard product are disposed to the 
existing Landfill. 
The Biomethanization Facility ha d an optimal capacity of 450 tons/day, where around 300 tons 
of biodegradable waste is utilized for year 2022.  The final capacity of the Biomethanization 
Facility will be reached by the upcoming  years. Maximum capacity of 180 tons/day (3.512 
m3/reactor) are built in the Biomethanization Facility.

• Anaerobic Reactors 
• Dewatering Unit and Liquid Fermented Product Storage  
• Solid Fermented Product Warehouse  
• Mixing System 
• Gas Storage Unit 
• Gas Purification System  
• Gas Engines 
• Booster Unit (with Flare)

1.13 Conditions Prior to Project Initiation 
Within the borders of İzmir Province, Bergama District, Kaşıkçı Neighborhood, Bergama 
Municipality took the decision of "Environmental Impacts are Insignificant" with the decision 
number 162/2002/02 and dated 28 -February-2002 and started to be operated by Bergama 
Municipality as "Bergama Regular Solid Waste Disposal Facility" in 2011. The facility continued 
to be operated by the Izmir Metropolitan Municipality as of 01 -October-2014 with the law 
numbered 6360. 
Prior to the proposed project activity, the wastes were left to decay within landfill site and 
methane was emitted to the atmosphere without any control or utilization.  
Please refer to Section 3.4 for further details on baseline scenario .

1.16.3 Supply Chain (Scope 3) Emissions  
In this project, emissions are reduced from the electricity sector in TÜRKİYE by implementing 
renewable energy sources and energy efficiency measures. The produced good in this project is 
electricity, which falls under Scope 1 and 2 emissions of industrial  producers. As such, there is 
no direct involvement of any specific producer or retailer of the impacted good or service in this 
project. 
Double counting is a common source of concern in the tracking and reporting of emissions 
reductions because it can result in overstated emissions reductions, misrepresentation of actual 
emissions, and undermine the integrity of the emissions reduction cert ification process. To 
address this concern, it is vital to have a robust and transparent tracking and reporting 
mechanism in place, which is exactly what we have done with our LFG project.  
TÜRKİYE has a centralized national grid system, which means that all the electricity generated 
by the project is fed into the same grid and distributed to all consumers. This is a crucial factor 
in avoiding the risk of double counting, as the electricity g enerated from our project is not 
separately metered and credited, but instead blended with other sources of electricity in the grid, 
so there is no way to determine the exact proportion of electricity generated from our project.

1.17 Sustainable Development Contributions 
1.17.1 Sustainable Development Contributions Activity Description 
The Project will contribute to sustainable development in the following ways:  
• Reduce CH 4 emission. The project will recover and destroy landfill gas that consists 
mainly of greenhouse gas methane and would otherwise be released directly into the 
atmosphere, effectively reducing greenhouse gas emissions, which contributes to the 
Turkey’s commitment to carbon dioxide emissions;  
• Increase electricity production from renewable sources, which will certainly reduce the 
consumption of fossil fuel in Turkish Power Grid and further help to achieve the national 
action to promote renewable energy development;  
• Improve the operational safety of the landfills. The project reduces the potential safety 
hazards of landfill gas explosion by recycling landfill gas and improves the safety of 
landfill operation;  
• The project will also provide new jobs opportunities and increase tax revenue, which will 
have a positive effect on the local economy.  
• Improving air quality (i.e. by reducing odor) and therefore having positive effects on the 
local environment.  
Nevertheless, it is still possible to note that the project will make positive contributions to at least 
six Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). These are:  
SDG Goal 5: Gender equality : Target 5.5, Ensure women’s full and effective participation and 
equal opportunities for leadership at all levels of decision making in political, economic and 
public life.  Sub indicator 5.5.2 description is “ Proportion of women in managerial positions ”. 
There is an initiative called “30 Percent Club” to promote women participation in the boards and 
top executive teams internationally and domestically.  Türkiye’s 30 Percent Club initiative’s aims 
are women participation in the boards and top executive teams of BIST Star Market, Main Market 
companies and BIST 100 companies 5. Biotrend company has joined the initiative 6 and number 
of women managers on board satisfy 30% condition 78. KPIs (5.5.1) to track the projects’ 
contribution to SDG 5 is number of women in managerial positions to total number of managerial

SDG Goal 7: Affordable and Clean Energy:  Target 7.2: Increase global percentage of renewable 
energy. Sub indicator 7.2.1 description is “ Renewable energy share in the total final energy 
consumption”. The proposed Project is a waste to power project that will generate renewable 
energy by capturing methane from municipal waste and utilizing it to produce thermal and electric 
energy through gas engine systems. By supplying renewable energy generated at th e plant to the 
national grid, the proposed Project will contribute to increasing the share of renewable energy in 
the global energy mix and the proportion of the population with  primary reliance on clean fuels 
and technology. KPIs (7.2.1) to track the projects’ contribution to SDG 7 is  amount of renewable 
energy supplied to grid for consumption.   
SDG Goal 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth : Target 8.5: Full employment and decent work 
with equal pay. The demand for food and electric energy is rapidly increasing in Turkey for various 
reasons, such as industrialization, urbanization, economic development, and population growth. 
The country's external dependence on agricultural products has intensified be cause the 
increasing demand cannot be met by a decreasing domestic agricultural production capacity 
which is due to several reasons, such as shrinkage  of agricultural land, increasing migration to 
urban spaces from rural areas where agricultural production is densely located, and dramatic 
increase in the cost of inputs for agricultural production. Therewithal, Turkey, which cannot meet 
its increasing el ectricity demand due to the deprivation of conventional resources used to 
generate energy, such as coal, oil, and natural gas, has also become foreign -dependent on 
energy. Through its implementation, the proposed project activity will contribute to reducin g 
Turkey's foreign dependency by generating renewable energy out of municipal wastes. In addition, 
as an LFG- based renewable energy technology implementation, the proposed project activity will 
achieve higher levels of economic productivity; hence, increa sing the annual growth rate of real 
GDP per employed person. Moreover, it will increase the region's employment capacity while 
decreasing the unemployment rate. KPIs (8.5.2) to track the projects’ contribution to SDG 8 via 
target 8.5 is  average earning of females and male employees engaged in the project and 
segregated by age and persons with disabilities.  According to 11th development plan of Türkiye9,

baseline is 38.5%.

1.18 Additional Information Relevant to the Project  
Leakage Management 
Leakage emissions are considered as per TOOL14: Project and leakage emissions from 
anaerobic digesters. Default value is adopted for pro ject emissions from biogas leak.  
Under project activity, LFG generated from landfill goes directly to gas engines where flare system 
can destroy excess gas during emergency situations.  There are multiple pressure sensors with 
digester and at the booster that can detect leaks based on unusual pressure drops. When such 
conditions occur, leak location is found by plant technicians and bypass lines continue operation. 
HDPE pipes allow leak repairs and gas balloon lets extra down-time for repairs. Under worst case 
where such measures cannot be applied for  generated biogas , safety valves can be used for 
venting extra biogas and electricity production would be halted. No such scenario realized before 
and plant operation is constantly monitored for maximum performance and leak prevention.  
Commercially Sensitive Information  
No commercially sensitive information has been excluded from the public version of the project 
description. 
Further Information 
There is no further information to be shared.  
2 SAFEGUARDS 
2.1 No Net Harm 
The Ministry of Environment and Urbanization has authorized the Environmental Impact 
Assessment (EIA) Report. According to Turkish rules and regulations, an EIA's permission may 
only be granted if the project in question has no detrimental effects on the e nvironment or the 
economy, thus the project has no net harm. All kinds of preventive measures will be taken to 
minimize the odor, dust, leachate, gas and similar negative effects that may arise from the facility. 
These preventive measures are explained in Environmental Impact Assessment Report in further 
detail. The landfill floor will be constructed in accordance with the provisions of the "Regulation 
on the Landfilling of Wastes" and the Circular on the Preparation of the Sanitary Landfills 
Implementation Project, numbered 2018/15, which was published in the Official Gazette dated 
26-March-2010 and numbered 27533. A letter of conformity will be obtained from the relevant 
institution regarding the impermeability of the sealed floors and the impermeable pool s 
throughout the project. If it is determined that the springs, fountains, groundwaters and 
boreholes in the vicinity of the activity area are adversely affected in terms of water quantity and 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
30 
quality, the activity will be stopped immediately, and the project will be terminated, and any 
damage will be borne by the activity owner.  
2.2 Local Stakeholder Consultation 
Within the scope of the Environmental Impact Assessment Regulation, which was published in 
the Official Gazette dated 25 -November-2014 and numbered 29186, a public participation 
meeting was held at the place and date determined by the Provincial Directorate of Environment 
and Urbanization in order to inform the public about the investment and to receive their opinions 
and suggestions regarding the project. The Public Participation Meeting was held on 12 -
December-2019, under the presidency of İzmir Provincial Directorate of Environment and 
Urbanization, in Sindel District, District Coffeehouse. Please note that the photo of the meeting 
is given below. 
 
Figure 5. Public Participation Meeting  
Before the meeting date, the meeting was announced in both local and regional newspapers. The 
newspaper announcements are given in the appendix.  
During the Public Participation Meeting, the environmental impacts of the Project were explained 
by the company representative who prepared the EIA report and the questions were answered. 
Information about the project was given with a visual presentation w hich included the 
environmental impacts that will occur during the construction and operation phases and the 
precautions to be taken.  
There is a system established by İzmir Novtek Enerji Elektrik Üretim A. Ş. for maintaining 
communication with internal and external shareholders. External shareholders can reach the 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
31 
company via website or phone numbers and use default templates for this specific mechanism. 
There is also a specific box for collecting complaints, suggestions, or comments in on-site offices. 
Logbooks will be used for continuous feedback from local stakeholders. A logbook was left to the 
Mukhtar where complaints and wishes can be conveyed. The Mukhtar will be visited and checked 
regularly. 
Plant managers are directly responsible for keeping communication with locals directly or through 
Mukhtars. The Human  resources department are responsible for communication with internal 
shareholders and corporate communication department is responsible for communicating with 
external shareholders.  
2.3 Environmental Impact 
An environmental impact assessment dated in 20 -August-2020 was prepared to assess the 
environmental effects of the project activity. The report has been prepared in accordance with 
Turkey’s national standards. Therefore, the project is implemented according to the national laws 
and regulations if the environm ental precautions stated in the report are applied. EIA has been 
conducted to assess all potential impacts and minimize through measures adopted.  
A public participation meeting was held on 12 -December-2019, within the scope of the 
Environmental Impact Assessment Regulation, which was published in the Official Gazette dated 
25-November-2014 and numbered 29186. The meeting’s purpose was to inform the public about 
the investment and to receive their opinions and suggestions regarding the project.  
A capacity increase, implementation of Refuse Drive Fuel production and exclusion of composting 
is described in EIA update which is undergoing bureaucratic review and can be reached with a 
search in official website 24. 
Environmental impact  assessment report is accepted on 11 -September-2020 with decision 
number of 53785661-220.01-E.191230. 
2.4 Public Comments 
There was great interest to the Public Participation Meeting held regarding the project. The 
meeting was started by İzmir Provincial Directorate of Environment and Urbanization EIA Branch 
Manager S. Deniz SEVİMOĞLU. During the meeting, the presentation was made by the EIA Report 
Expert regarding the characteristics and environmental effects of the project was completed, and 
the public's opinions and suggestions were received after the presentation.  
During the meeting;  
• It was asked whether if similar facilities exist elsewhere in Turkey, and it was informed 
that the largest facility in this regard is in Ankara, Istanbul and Antalya.

• It has been said that there is no such thing as clean energy and that somehow the 
environment is harmed; Regarding this issue, it has been stated that no waste will be 
incinerated within the scope of the project, energy will be obtained from the methane gas 
that will be generated and there will be no environmental impact like the incineration 
plants. 
• It was asked whether there is any data on how much methane gas will be generated in 
the facility, and it was stated that methane gas that will be generated during the storage 
of garbage will be combusted, thus preventing the release of methane gas to the

• It has been stated that the local people have concerns about the formation of an odor 
originating from the facilities, and it has been stated that the facilities will be planned in 
a completely closed manner, they will be made much better than the current situation 
and all necessary precautions will be taken.  
The meeting was ended without any problems by the İzmir Directorate of Environment and 
Urbanization and the meeting was recorded in the minutes.  
The project was open for public comment from 16-January-2023 to 15-February-2023.25 No

Applicability of ACM0001: Flaring or use of landfill gas --- Version 19.0 
Applicability Condition  Justification 
Paragraph 2 states (Scope) : 
This methodology applies to project activities 
that include the destruction of methane 
emissions and displacement of a more -GHG-
intensive service by capturing landfill gas 
from the landfill site and/or flaring and/or 
using to produce energy (i.e. electrici ty, 
thermal energy); and/or using to supply 
consumers through natural gas distribution 
network, dedicated pipeline or trucks  
Paragraph 2:  
Applicable. Project activity is the destruction 
of methane emissions and displacement of a 
more-GHG-intensive service by capturing 
landfill gas from the landfill site and 
generating electricity. 
Paragraph 3 states: 
The methodology is applicable under the 
following conditions: 
(a) Install a new LFG capture system in 
an existing or new (Greenfield) SWDS 
where no LFG capture system was or 
would have been installed prior to the 
implementation of the project activity; 
or 
Paragraph 3:  
a) Prior to the project activity, there was 
a vent system that allows LFG to be vented to 
the atmosphere. LFG capture and utilization 
system was implemented with the project 
activity. Thus, this criterion is applicable.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
39 
(b) Make an investment into an existing 
LFG capture system to increase the 
recovery rate or change the use of the 
captured LFG, provided that:  
(i) The captured LFG was vented or 
flared and not used prior to the 
implementation of the project activity; 
and 
(ii) In the case of an existing active LFG 
capture system for which the amount 
of LFG cannot be collected separately 
from the project system after the 
implementation of the project activity 
and its efficiency is not impacted on 
by the project system: historical  data 
on the amount of LFG capture and 
flared is available; 
(c) Flare the LFG and/or use the 
captured LFG in any (combination) of 
the following ways: 
(i) Generating electricity;  
(ii) Generating heat in a boiler, air heater 
or kiln (brick firing only) or glass 
melting furnace; and/or  
(iii) Supplying the LFG to consumers 
through a natural gas distribution 
network; 
(iv) Supplying compressed/liquefied LFG 
to consumers using trucks;  
(v) Supplying the LFG to consumers 
through a dedicated pipeline;  
Do not reduce the amount of organic waste 
that would be recycled in the absence of the 
project activity. 
b) i) Captured LFG was vented to the 
atmosphere before the implementation of 
project activity. 
Thus, this criterion is applicable.  
Criterion (ii) does not apply because there 
was no existing active LFG capture system 
prior to the implementation of the project 
activity.  
c) Captured LFG is used for generating 
electricity. Thus, criteria (i) is applicable. 
Criteria (ii), (iii), (iv) and (v) are not 
applicable. Flaring system is a part of 
booster units and designed to be operational 
only during emergencies such as failure of 
gas engines for extensive period of time.  
d) The organic waste that was brought 
to the site was sent to landfill and was not 
used for any purpose. As such there was no 
diversion of organic waste prior to project 
implementation. Furthermore, no diversion of 
organic waste has occurred during the 
project implementation. Thus, this criterion is 
applicable. 
Paragraph 4 states:  
(a) Atmospheric release of the LFG or 
capture of LFG and destruction through 
flaring to comply with regulations or 
contractual requirements, to address safety 
and odour concerns, or for other reasons; 
and  
Paragraph 4: 
(a) Applicable. The most feasible baseline 
scenario of the project is (a): Atmospheric 
release of the LFG; and for electricity 
generation: that electricity would be 
generated in the grid. Regulation on 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2

be generated in the grid or in cap tive fossil 
fuel fired power plants; and/or (ii) For heat

3.3 Project Boundary 
According to the ACM0022, the spatial extent of the project boundary is the SWDS where the 
waste is disposed of in the baseline, anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits treating organic wastewater 
in the baseline, and the site of the alternative waste treatment process(es). The boundary also 
includes on -site electricity generation and use, on -site fuel use and the wastewater treatment 
plant used to treat the w astewater by-products of the alternative waste treatment process(es). 
The project boundary does not include facilities for waste collection and transport.  
In the case that the project provides electricity to a grid, then the spatial extent of the project 
boundary will also include those plants connected to the energy system to which the plant is 
connected. If upgraded biogas is fed to a natural gas distribut ion system, then the natural gas 
distribution system is also included in the boundary.

Section 5.3 of applied methodology, titled “Procedure for estimating the end of the remaining 
lifetime of existing equipment” describes the procedure for used equipment.  
Paragraph 17 states: “ This procedure applies if LFG is used in equipment that was in operation 
prior to the implementation of the project activity. ”. Under project activity, LFG is only used in gas 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
63 
engines and flare. Both units are purchased exclusively for the project activity and not used in 
any facility before.  
Paragraph 18 states: “ For each item of equipment which was in operation prior to the 
implementation of the project activity and in which the captured LFG is used after the 
implementation of the project activity, project participants shall estimate its remaining lifetime 
by appl ying the “Tool to determine the remaining lifetime of equipment 30”. These items of 
equipment and their remaining lifetime shall be recorded in the CDM -PDD.”  Since all equipment 
that uses LFG has not been used in other projects before, this paragraph is not applicable.  
Paragraph 19 states: “ At the end of the remaining lifetime of each item of equipment, the 
procedure for the selection of the most plausible baseline scenario related to electricity and/or 
heat generation shall be updated in order to determine the most plausible baseline fuel that 
would be used after installation of the new equipment in the absence of the CDM project activity. 
At the same time, the parameters related to this item of equipmen t shall also be re -estimated 
according to the procedures in this met hodology used to make the original estimation (for 
example the baseline fuel may change, and this then has impacts on the emission factor for this 
baseline fuel).”.  
According to TOOL 10 of CDM, titled “Tool to determine the remaining lifetime of equipment” 
version 01 31 , default technical lifetime of gas fired generator sets are 50000h. This value is 
roughly equivalent to 7 years of operation. However, in practice with good maintenance, 
optimization and part replacement life time of equipment can be expanded. Project owner plans 
to use the gas engines for a duration of 15-20 years. Although, it is not expected for gas engine 
and flare to outlast the project activity (even without the renewal), project will evaluate the most 
plausible baseline scenario related to electricity and/or heat generation shall be updated in order 
to determine the most plausible baseline fuel that would be used after installation of the new 
equipment in the absence of the CDM project activity. At the sa me time, the parameters related 
to this item of equipment shall also be re -estimated according to the procedures in this 
methodology used to make the original estimation . 
 
3.4 Baseline Scenario 
According to ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment processes --- Version 03.0, the following 
alternatives or combinations of these alternatives shall, inter alia, be considered for the baseline 
scenario of the fresh waste treatment:  
(a) M1: The project activity without being registered as a CDM project activity (i.e. any 
(combination) of the waste treatment processes listed in Table 2);  
 
30 https://cdm.unfccc.int/methodologies/PAmethodologies/tools/am -tool-10-v1.pdf

(b) M2: Disposal of the fresh waste in a SWDS with a partial capture of the LFG and flaring 
of the captured LFG;  
(c) M3: Disposal of the fresh waste in a SWDS without a LFG capture system;  
(d) M4: Part of the fresh fraction of the solid waste is recycled and not disposed in the SWDS; 
(e) M5: Part of the fresh fraction of the solid waste is treated aerobically and not disposed 
in the SWDS; 
(f) M6: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is incinerated and not disposed in the 
SWDS; 
(g) M7: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is gasified and not disposed in the 
SWDS; 
(h) M8: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is treated in an anaerobic digester and 
not disposed in the SWDS;  
(i) M9: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is mechanically or thermally treated to 
produce RDF/SB and not disposed in the SWDS.  
There is no national regulation which would require the recovery or flaring of the LFG emitted 
from landfill sites. The relevant environmental legislation does not force municipalities to 
effectively control LFG emissions from landfills; hence, it is rathe r a tender for the rights to use 
the site for power generation purposes. The LFG is not being flared. Since Turkey has no 
regulation to enforce landfill gas capture, this scenario is considered realistic. However electricity 
generation from landfills is applied in the other landfills of the province, thus this is a conservative 
and realistic scenario. Anaerobic digestion treatment method adopted in this project is 
encouraged by zero waste perspective  in Turkey, therefore, M1 is a  feasible baseline scenario.  
Thus, baseline scenario M1, M2 and M3 are applicable. 
For electricity generation the following alternatives or combinations of these alternatives, inter 
alia, shall be considered:  
(a) P1: Electricity generated as an output of one of the waste treatment processes listed in 
Table 1, not undertaken as a CDM project activity;  
(b) P2: Use of an existing or construction of a new on -site or off -site fossil fuel fired 
cogeneration plant;  
(c) P3: Existing or new construction of an on -site or off -site renewable based cogeneration 
plant; 
(d) P4: Existing or new construction of an on -site or off-site fossil fuel fired electricity plant;  
(e) P5: Existing or new construction of an on-site or off-site renewable based electricity plant; 
(f) P6: Electricity generation in existing and/or new grid -connected electricity plants.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
65 
Before the project activity, the project area was already being used as sanitary landfill.  However, 
there was no destruction of methane neither by flaring nor by electricity production.  Therefore, 
P6 is a plausible scenario for project activity. Greenfield facility is connected to grid where there 
was no power generation from neither fossil fuel nor renewable sources.  
For the treatment of the wastewater, the following alternatives or combinations of these 
alternatives shall, inter alia, be considered:  
 
(a) W1: Continuation of current practice of using anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits without 
methane recovery;  
(b) W2: Anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits with methane recovery and flaring of the recovered 
methane;  
(c) W3: Anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits with methane recovery and utilization of the recovered 
methane for electricity and/or heat generation;  
(d) W4: Construction of a new anaerobic lagoon or sludge pits without methane recovery;  
(e) W5: Construction of a new anaerobic lagoon or sludge pits with methane recovery and flaring 
of the recovered methane;  
(f) W6: Using the wastewater for co -composting (the project activity implemented without being 
registered as a CDM project activity);  
(g) W7: Other treatment processes provided in table 6.3, Volume 5, chapter 6 of the IPCC 2006 
guidelines for greenhouse gas inventory.  
 
Treatment of the wastewater  is not included in the project activity, thus none of the options are 
applicable for baseline scenarios.  
For heat generation, one or a combination of the following alternatives, inter alia, shall be 
considered: 
(a) H1: Heat generated as a by -product from one of the processes for waste treatment listed in

cogeneration plant(s);5  
(c) H3: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on -site or off -site renewable based

According to paragraph 20 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ Project participants shall 
demonstrate that the identified baseline fuel used for generation of heat is available in 
abundance in the host country and there is no supply constraint. In case of supply constraints 
(e.g. partial or seasonal supply), project p articipants shall consider the period when there are 
availability constraints and choose the fuel(s) that result in the most conservative estimation of 
baseline emissions.”. Since project activity does not include heat generation, this condition is not 
applicable.  
According to paragraph 21 of the applied methodology ACM0022,  “Detailed justifications shall 
be provided and documented in the CDM -PDD for the selected baseline fuel. As a conservative 
approach, the lowest carbon -intensive fuel, such as natural gas, may be used throughout all 
periods of the year.”. Since project activity does not include heat generation, this condition is not 
applicable.  
According to paragraph 22 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “For the supply of upgraded 
biogas to a natural gas distribution network, the baseline is assumed to be the continued supply 
with natural gas. ”. Since project activity does not include supply of upgraded biogas to a natural 
gas distribution network, this condition is not applicable.  
According to paragraph 23 of the applied methodology ACM0022,  “In applying Sub -step 1b of 
the tool, mandatory applicable legal and regulatory requirements may include mandatory LFG 
capture or destruction requirements because of safety issues or local environmental regulations. 
Other policies could include local polici es promoting productive use of LFG, such as those for the 
production of renewable energy, or those that promote the processing of fresh waste.”. In Türkiye 
there are no laws and regulations tha t require landfills to flare or utilize generated landfill gas. 
According to paragraph 24 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “If the products or by-products 
are used by another CDM project activity and if the project participants of both CDM projects are 
the same, then the following provision shall be followed:  
 
(a) The by-products shall be considered to have market value when assessing additionality of the 
project activity applying this methodology;  
(b) The by -products shall be considered to have no value when assessing additionality of the 
second project activity.”

automatically additional when  any of conditions below is fulfilled: (a) The host country is a LDC; 
or  
(b) MSW collection coverage is below 50 per cent for the applicable geographical region; or  
(c) MSW collection coverage is 50 –80 per cent for the applicable geographical region and the 
waste received by the project composting facility does not have formal segregation of wet and 
dry waste (i.e. excluding recycling by the informal sector); or  
(d) No gate/tipping fees are received by the project composting facility or any upstream waste 
collection or segregation facility which is contractually bound to provide waste to the project 
composting facility; or  
(e) Less than two per cent of the collected MSW of the municipality (or the region from where the 
MSW treated by the project activity is sourced) are treated by composting in the year before the 
PDD is published for global stakeholder consultation. ”  
Since project activity does not include composting or co -composting, this condition is not 
applicable. 
According to paragraph 26 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ For project activities that are 
deemed automatically additional above, the baseline scenario is assumed to be the continued 
disposal of the fresh waste in the nearest SWDS (where the LFG is released or captured and 
destructed through flaring to comply with  regulations or contractual requirements, to address 
safety and odour concerns, or for other reasons). If condition (d) or (e) applies, it shall also be 
demonstrated that 80 per cent of the collected MSW of the municipality (or the region from where 
the MSW treated by the project activity is sourced) are treated in SWDS or stockpiles or are 
dumped in the year before the PDD is published for global stakeholder consultation. ”  
Project activities that are associated with methodology ACM0022 are not automatically 
additional. Therefore, this condition is not applicable.  
According to paragraph 27 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ The collection coverage of 
MSW may be estimated as: (1) the quantity of waste collected divided by the total waste 
generation, or (2) the population covered by waste collection service divided by the total 
population. The quantity of waste collected may be  (1) obtained from the municipal waste 
authority, (2) based on local statistics, or (3) based on the MSW accepted by all waste processing 
facilities, including open dump sites. Total waste gene ration may be calculated using data on 
population and/or waste generation per capita. ”.

According to ACM00 01: Flaring or use of landfill gas --- Version 19.0 section 5.3, project 
participants may either apply the simplified procedure in section 5.3.1 or the procedures in 
section 5.3.2 to select the most plausible baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality.  
The simplified procedure is applied for the proposed project, and the baseline is assumed to be 
the atmospheric release of the LFG; all the electricity to be supplied by the project is assumed to 
be electricity generation in existing and/or new grid -connected power plants. 
According to paragraph 22 of the applied methodology ACM0001, “The baseline scenario for LFG 
is assumed to be the atmospheric release of the LFG or capture of LFG and destruction through 
flaring to comply with regulations or contractual requirements, to ad dress safety and odour 
concerns, or for other reasons”. The project baseline scenario for LFG is “the atmospheric release 
of the LFG”. 
Before the project activity, the landfill site was a sanitary landfill where biomass and other organic 
matter content of MSW is  left to decay within the project boundary, and generated methane 
emitted to the atmosphere in the absence of the project activity. There were no flaring nor energy 
generation at the landfill site. 
According to paragraph 23 of the applied methodology ACM0001, “If all or part of the electricity 
generated by the project activity is exported to the grid, the baseline scenario for all or the part 
of the electricity exported to the grid is assumed to be e lectricity generation in existing and/or 
new grid-connected power plants. If all or part of the electricity is supplied to off -grid application, 
the baseline electricity generation equipment is assumed to correspond to the default emission 
factor from Opti on B2 of the "Methodological tool: Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions 
from electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity generation”.  
According to this explanation, the most plausible baseline scenario for electricity generation is 
P6 “electricity generation in existing and/or new grid -connected power plants”.

There are no technology barriers, acceptability barriers nor financial barriers that may prevent 
these two alternative scenarios from occurring. As per Tool 02 Combined tool to identify the

STEP 3 – Investment analysis;  
The investment analysis has been done in order to make an economic and financial evaluation 
of the project. No public funding or ODA are available in Turkey for finance of this type of projects. 
For investment analysis, loan conditions have been determined  considering the average market 
rates/term sheets signed with the banks. Investment analysis is carried as per the 
methodological Tool 2: “ Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate 
additionality”, Version 07.0.  
Sub-step 2a - Determine appropriate analysis method  
There are three options for the determination of analysis method which are:  
• Simple Cost Analysis  
• Investment Comparison Analysis and  
• Benchmark Analysis 
Since Project generates economic benefits from sales of electricity, the simple cost analysis is 
not applicable. Also, since the baseline of the project is generation of electricity by the grid, no 
alternative investment is considered at issue. So, it has been decided to use benchmark analysis 
for evaluation of the project investment.  
Sub-step 2b: Option III. Apply benchmark analysis  
For benchmark analysis, figure defined by World Bank for similar project types have been used 
which has been given as 20% (pre -tax)37 for IRR by a report generated in June 2017. For the 
proposed project, in order to reach this IRR values, average electricity tariff must be above current

The technical lifetime of Bergama integrated solid waste management facility and electricity

calculation the date specified in the tool was used .  
The World Bank has established a standard for renewable energy investments in Turkey. It has 
an IRR threshold benchmark, which is the minimum IRR necessary for project finance. As a result, 
it is a "commercial lending rate," as defined by T OOL27. The benchmark IRR was derived from a 
World Bank loan to the Turkish Renewable Energy Sector under the Clean Technology Fund (CTF). 
The suggested CTF benchmark was found to be compatible with the qualifying requirements for 
emission reduction projects ( that is, sign ificant potential in emission reductions, demonstration 
potential, development impact and implementation potential). For each project type, threshold 
IRRs have been computed, which are the lowest IRRs required to attract investors.  
Based on an analysis of prototype sub -projects (see Table 3.3 page 40), it was determined that 
CTF resources would be made available for newer RE technologies and EE investments, and for

Sub-step 2d – Sensitivity Analysis  
Sensitivity analysis was carrie d out for three main parameters identified for the first phase of the 
project. Since the investment cost of the project is not changed, only impact of change in tariff 
and operating cost has been included in sensitivity analysis.  
While the main parameter determining the income of the project is the electricity sales revenue, 
investment cost and operation cost, a variation of the accordant values shall demonstrate the 
reliability of the IRR calculation. Applying variations on invest ment costs, the range was 
considered to be in a bandwidth of +10% and –10% as advised in “Tool27 - Investment Analysis 
(ver 07.0).” 
The sensitivity analysis confirms that the proposed project activity is unlikely to be economically 
attractive without the revenues from VER as even the maximum IRR result ( 13.540%) for the 
best-case scenario is below the benchmark, which is 10%. In this scenario, increase of revenue 
up to %10 is limited for electricity revenue by the local regulation discussed below in details. 
Electricity revenues have majority in the total revenues however other revenues are also effective 
where they depend on the waste amount which are fixed by agreements. Also, for electricity 
revenue, according to YEKDEM pricing, the fixed tar iff is determined for a 10 years period. Since 
there is a fixed price and following to it ceiling price for renewable energy power plants, and waste 
amount which were already determined by agreements there is no chance that the revenue will 
be +10%. 
The project has already financed with project owner equity and due to the fact that the project 
owner is the distributor of the electronic components in Turkey so, own resources are used in this 
project that means there is no chance that the investment cos t will be -%10. In addition to that, 
Turkey has feed in tariff which means fixed electricity price for renewable project, it is unlikely to 
have a higher electricity price, which is above the market price.

1,200 TL/ MWh for renewables and 2,500 TL/ MWh for fossil -fueled power plants. At the time of 
announcement those values are approximately 80 USD/MWh for renewables and 120 USD/MWh 
for fossil-fueled plants. The price difference between the barrier and PTF price will be charged as

for coal and natural gas -based power plants. Although this practice is designed to protect and 
ensure the deman d for energy and secure consumers, it will directly harm the producers, 
especially renewable energy -based producers

4 IMPLEMENTATION STATUS 
4.1 Implementation Status of the Project Activity 
 
The project site is located within the boundaries of the II. Class Bergama Solid Waste Landfill 
Facility and Landfill gas (LFG) to power facility integrated into the existing II. Class Solid Waste 
Landfill Facility.  
Waste acceptance to the sanitary landfill started in 2011. The facility is originally planned to 
include two additional lots. Originally landfill was planned to accept MSW from only Bergama and 
Dikili districts however in time MSW is accepted from more districts. Izmir province has an aging 
major landfill called Harmandalı and its slowly fulfilling its lifespan. Therefore, more MSW is 
directed to Bergama than original plan, including Kınık District and Aliağa District. The daily waste 
quantum of 435 tons/day (2020) is planned to reach 1200 tons/day in 2027. This situation is 
reflected in the recent EIA revision where a third lot is licensed.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
80 
 
Figure 7. Flow Diagram of Facility  
In 2014, The Bergama Solid Waste Disposal Facility was handed over to the Izmir Metropolitan 
Municipality. In 2019 Izmir Metropolitan Municipality handed over the operation and 
responsibility of modern solid waste management systems installation to the  İzmir Doğu Star 
Elektrik Üretim A.Ş  which is sub -company of Biotrend. After the handover, the facility was 
transformed into an Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility. Mechanical Separation Facility, 
Biomethanization Facility, Compost Facility and Electricity Generation Facilities were a dded. 
In line with the sustainable integrated waste management planned within the scope of the 
project, the packaging wastes are collected separately at the source and sent to the collection 
and sorting facilities licensed by the Ministry of Environment and Urba nization, and they will be 
sorted according to their types there. Packaging waste to be collected separately at their source 
will not be accepted to the facility or any sanitary landfill.  
The project, Bergama Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility and Electricity Generation 
Facility (10.157 MWm / 9.898 MWe / 13.895 MWt) is in Izmir Province, Bergama District, Kaşıkçı 
Neighborhood, and owned and operated by İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik Üretim A.Ş.  
The project site is located within the boundaries of the II. Class Bergama Solid Waste Landfill 
Facility. It is planned to integrate the facilities to be established within the scope of the project 
and the existing II. Class Solid Waste Landfill Facility.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
81 
After the mixed municipal wastes coming to the facility are separated according to their types at 
the Mechanical Separation Facility, the biodegradable wastes will be taken to the 
Biomethanization Facility, the remaining wastes will be sent to the existing  sanitary landfill and 
the recyclable wastes will be sent to licensed companies. It’s estimated that about 20% of the 
biodegradable material cannot be separated and sent to landfill. The biogas to be obtained from 
the Biomethanization Facility and the meth ane gas to be extracted from the sanitary landfill will 
be converted into electrical energy at the Electricity Generation Facility and transferred to the 
interconnected system to generate income.  
In line with the sustainable integrated waste management planned within the scope of the 
project, the packaging wastes to be collected separately at the source will be sent to the 
collection and sorting facilities licensed by the Ministry of Environment an d Urbanization, and 
they will be sorted according to their types there. Packaging waste to be collected separately at 
their source will not be accepted to the facility or any sanitary landfill.  
RDF production  and capacity increase for mechanical separation units are expected to be in 
service as of January 2024 therefore RDF process is also accounted for after the 
beforementioned date. 
It's estimated that at full capacity, annual net electricity generation will reach 74,235 MWh and 
corresponding annual estimated emission reduction is 196,867 tCO2e as average. The starting 
date of the project activity shall be considered as 31 -October-2020 which is the provisional 
acceptance date of the first two gas engines.  
The Ministry of Environment and Urbanization has authorized the Environmental Impact 
Assessment (EIA) Report. According to Turkish rules and regulations, an EIA's permission may 
only be granted if the project in question has no detrimental effects on the e nvironment or the 
economy, thus the project has no net harm. All kinds of preventive measures will be taken to 
minimize the odor, dust, leachate, gas and similar negative effects that may arise from the facility. 
These preventive measures are explained in the Environmental  Impact Assessment Report in 
further detail.  
There were delays with data recording during first crediting period and flowmeter assigned to 
power generation from only LFG stopped gas counting on 13 -April-2023. Data substitution used 
is used to fill gaps for intermittent data recording, however no base line emissions from 
methane at SWDS is counted for LFG after 13 -April-2023. 
Biogas generated by digesters are counted at two locations, first at the outlet of anaerobic 
digester and then with the booster unit. Amount of methane produced by digester is calculated 
with the flow meters placed at the outlet of digesters and, used for calculations of project and 
leakage emissions.

but require the installation of a system to capture and flare the LFG, then it is assumed f y = 0.2.

monitoring of electricity generation” . BEEN,t,y is equal to BEEC,y and baseline emissions from

methodology;  
 
b. In the tool, x begins with the year that the SWDS started receiving wastes (e.g. the 
first year of SWDS operation); and

Determination of leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate (LE storage,y) 
This step applies in the case that the digestate is stored under the following anaerobic conditions: 
(a) In an un-aerated lagoon that has a depth of more than one meter; or  
(b) In a SWDS, including stockpiles that are considered a SWDS as per the definitions 
section. 
Storage of digestate under anaerobic conditions can cause CH 4 emissions due to further 
anaerobic digestion of the residual biodegradable organic matter. The procedure for determining 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 is distinguished for liquid digestate and solid digestate.

Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB (LE RDF_SB,y) 
According to methodology ACM0022 v03.0 , Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB are 
accounted for the organic waste by -products of the treatment process (not by -products from the 
RDF/SB combustor), which may be composted or disposed of in a SWDS, and the end -use of 
RDF/SB that is exported off -site, as follows: 
𝐿𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = 𝐿𝐸𝐸𝑁𝐷𝑈𝑆𝐸_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 + 𝐿𝐸𝑆𝑊𝐷𝑆,𝑊𝐵𝑃_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 Equation (33) 
 
Where: 
LERDF_SB,y  =Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2e) 
LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y  =Leakage emissions associated with disposing of waste by products 
associated with RDF/SB production in a SWDS in year y (t CO 2e) 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   =Leakage emissions associated with the end -use of RDF/SB exported 
outside the project boundary in year y (t CO 2e) 
Leakage emissions from disposal of waste by -products from RDF/SB production in a SWDS 
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y is determined using “TOOL04: Emissions 
from solid waste disposal sites”. In the tool, x begins with the start of the CDM project activity and 
extends to the end of year y (e.g. emissions are calculated using Application B in the tool and waste 
disposed from the start of the first crediting period shall be considered). And Wj,x in the tool is the 
amount of organic waste contained in the waste by-products from the production of RDF/SB in year 
y (e.g. it do es not include waste by -products that are composted instead of being disposed to a 
SWDS in the project activity or waste by-products from the combustion of RDF/SB). 
RDF production is solely based on using certain fraction of municipal solid waste that is 
mechanically separated and mechanically processed on wet basis. Within mechanical separation 
processes, 2D wastes separated after ballistic separation recyclable materials are separated. 
Remaining material is converted into RDF and all of it considered and expected to be sold to the 
end-user. Output material contains organic fraction as well inorganic fraction (such as plastics, 
glass etc.). 
Based on this situation, no amount of by-product or trash is expected to be disposed of. Therefore  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
105 
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y) is considered zero. 
 
Leakage emissions associated with end use of RDF/SB exported outside the project boundary 
(LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, the potential leakage emissions associated with the use of 
the RDF/SB that is exported outside the project boundary are that it may be combusted or 
decompose anaerobically has to be accounted for. Emissions are therefore calculated allowing for 
the situation that RDF/SB exported in year y may have three different end uses: 
 
• End use 1: documented evidence is provided that the RDF/SB exported off-site is used as 
raw material in fertilizer, ceramic manufacture or as a fuel that is combusted in a CDM 
project activity. In this case, no leakage emissions are estimated; 
• End use 2: documented evidence is provided that the RDF/SB exported off-site is 
combusted or used as a raw material in furniture: In this case, the RDF/SB is considered 
to be combusted and LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y shall be calculated, according to procedure below; 
𝐿𝐸𝐸𝑁𝐷𝑈𝑆𝐸_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = 𝑄𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝐶𝑂𝑀,𝑦  𝑥 𝑁𝐶𝑉𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦  𝑥 𝐸𝐹𝐶𝑂2_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦  Equation (34) 
Where: 
 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   = Leakage emissions of CO 2 from off-site combustion of RDF/SB in year 
y (t CO2) 
QRDF_SB,COM,y   = Quantity of RDF/SB exported off -site with potential to be combusted 
in year y (t) 
EFCO2_RDF_SB,y  =CO2 emissions factor for RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2/GJ) 
NCVRDF_SB,y  = Net calorific value of RDF/SB in year y (GJ/t)

𝑊𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑗,𝑥,𝑎𝑑𝑗 = 𝑄𝑒𝑥𝑝𝑜𝑟𝑡,𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦
𝑄𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦
× 𝑊𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑗,𝑥 Equation (35)

disposed of in a SWDS (t)

5.4 Estimated Net GHG Emission Reductions and Removals 
To calculate the emission reductions the project participant shall apply the following equation:  
𝐸𝑅𝑦 = 𝐵𝐸𝑦 − 𝑃𝐸𝑦 − 𝐿𝐸𝑦 (Equation 36) 
Where: 
𝐸𝑅𝑦 = Emissions reductions in year y (t CO2e) 
𝐵𝐸𝑦 = Baseline emissions in year y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝑦 = Project emissions in the year y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑦 = Leakage emissions in year y (t CO2e)

For baseline emissions: according to the “Tool to determine

Gas Inventories (2006). The following values are to be considered: 
1.0 for anaerobic managed solid waste disposal sites. These must 
have controlled placement of waste (i.e., waste directed to specific 
deposition areas, a degree of control of scavenging and a degree

waste; 
0.5 for semi -aerobic managed solid waste disposal sites. These 
must have controlled placement of waste and will include all of the 
following structures for introducing air to waste layer: (i) permeable 
cover material; (ii) leachate drainage system; (iii) r egulating 
pondage; and (iv) gas ventilation system;  
0.8 for unmanaged solid waste disposal sites ñ deep and/or with 
high water table. This comprises all SWDS not meeting the criteria

Electricity meters calibration interval is 10 years 56. 
Purpose of data  Calculation of baseline emissions  
Calculation method  Net electricity generation values can be obtained from meter 
readings by PO and the official settlement records. It has been 
observed that net electricity generation value is more 
conservative than the difference of Gross electricity generation 
and Electri city consumption. Therefore, values from the 
settlement records are used due to them being more conservative 
and accurate. UEVM (Settlement record net electricity supply) 
value from settlement records is used as gross electricity 
generation and UEÇM (Corre ction factor) value from settlement 
records. Invoicing is also based on settlement records.  
Comments  This parameter is required for calculating baseline emissions 
associated with electricity generation (BE EC,y) using the 
methodological tool “Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions 
from electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity 
generation”

measurement methods 
and procedures applied  
For each equipment unit j using the LFG monitor that the plant is 
operating in hour h by the monitoring any one or more of the 
following three parameters:  
Temperature. Determine the location for temperature 
measurements and minimum operational temperature based on 
manufacturer’s specifications of the burning equipment. 
Document and justify the location and minimum threshold in the 
PDD; 
Flame. Flame detection system is used to ensure that the 
equipment is in operation;  
Products generated. Monitor the generation  
Opj,h = 0 when: 
One of more temperature measurements are missing below the 
minimum threshold in hour h (instantaneous measurements are 
made at least every minute);  
Flame is not detected continuously in hour h (instantaneous 
measurements are made at least every minute)  
Otherwise, O pj,h = 1

QA/QC procedures to be 
applied According to the document “CDM validation and verification 
standard for project activities” Version 03.0, paragraph 366. (a); 
When the calibration period is delayed, following procedure has 
to be applied: 
a) Applying the maximum permissible error of 
the instrument to the measured values taken 
during the period between the scheduled 
date of calibration and the actual date of 
calibration, if the results of the delayed 
calibration do not show any errors in the 
measuring equipment, or if the error is 
smaller than the maximum permissible error  
b) Applying the error identified in the delayed 
calibration test, if the error is beyond the 
maximum permissible error of the measuring 
equipment. 
According to device technical specification 59, maximum 
permissible error rate is 0.075% 60. When calibration is delayed, 
0.075% device accuracy rate will be subtracted from methane 
content reading.

6.3 Monitoring Plan 
The responsible entity for the monitoring system is Biotrend personnel. The monitoring activities 
will primarily involve four types of personnel: the Operation Site Manager, Field Operator, 
Electrical & Mechanical Operator, and the Laboratory Operator.  
The Field Operators will perform activities such as monitoring and adjusting LFG extraction wells, 
checking operations of the blower and flare, recording data at the blower/flare station, routine
