148 
maintenance of collection system components, preparing daily logs and completing checklists, 
and send data to the Operation Site Manager.  
The Operation Manager’s responsibilities include reviewing the data collected both manually by 
the Field Technicians and the one recorded automatically by analytical equipment, making 
recommendations and/or implementing system adjustments to maximize metha ne capture and 
destruction, scheduling monitoring and O&M activities, performing quality assurance checks on 
operations, coordinating with system component manufacturers as needed, to maintain proper 
operations and calibration, and compiling data as requir ed by the Methodology. 
Only for manual data collection, the Operation Manager will be responsible for reviewing the data 
collected. 
Project Management Responsibility  
The operators of the LFG recovery and electricity generation system will be responsible for 
collecting all data monitored on -site. The operating and maintenance personnel will be skilled 
technicians, with extensive experience in equipment operation, maintenanc e and calibration, 
and emergency procedures. Overall responsibility for the monitoring and maintenance of all 
required tasks and their adequate management lie with the project manager. Detailed roles and 
responsibilities of the relevant staff involved in V CS monitoring are placed.  
Training of Monitoring Personnel  
The monitoring personnel is trained in the beginning of the project; the purpose of this training 
is to operate the project in a well manner. Periodical training will be defined by Biotrend.  
 
Organizational Structure of the Carbon Project  
 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
149 
Data Analysis 
The collected data will be reviewed and analyzed on a daily basis by the Plant Managers and on 
weekly basis by sustainability manager . In case of a drift of one parameter the Manager or 
Technician can react quickly to fix potential problems. All data required for the emission reduction 
calculations will be kept in the onsite - monitoring database. The Monitoring Manager will be the 
responsible to report the necessary information to Biotrend.  
The quality actions that will guarantee the success of the monitoring plan are the following:  
Maintenance Plan 
The LFG plant's computerized management and control system includes: the management of 
level management in gas meters through the level indicators; monitoring and control of the 
heating system; alarm management of minimum and maximum levels and security; a nd 
monitoring of the data analysis of the LFG analysis. With regards to the electricity generation 
component of the project, given that it dispatches electricity to the National Grid, measuring, 
recording, storing, aggregating, collating, and reporting dat a and parameters will follow the 
procedure described by the authorities.  
The following aspects are core to the maintenance of the monitoring system in order to assure 
proper data monitoring during the project:  
• Equipment preventive maintenance  
• Equipment calibration  
All data collected as part of monitoring will be archived electronically and be kept at least for 2 
years after the end of the last crediting period. Regarding the monitoring equipment of the 
methane recovery component of the project, maintenance and calib ration will be performed in 
line with manufacturers’ recommendations. With regards to the electricity meter, it is a high 
accuracy measurement device/s and meets all relevant metrological requirements prescribed by 
the state authority. Procedures for maint enance of the meter will be conducted in accordance 
with national procedures and standards.  
Quality assurance and Control (QA/QC)  
Quality control and quality assurance procedures will guarantee the quality of monitored data.  
All data is archived electronically, backed up regularly and kept at least for 2 years after the end 
of the last crediting period. There are corrective actions when the parameters are out of the 
permitted range. 
The gas engines have their own monitoring system and parameters can be seen every time on 
the display. There is an internal storage of the data on PC and on the cloud. All data can be 
downloaded in an excel file.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
150 
Regarding the monitoring equipment of the methane recovery component of the project, 
maintenance and calibration will be performed in line with manufacturers’ recommendations. 
With regards to the electricity meter, as mentioned above, it is a high accuracy  measurement 
device/s and meets all relevant metrological requirements prescribed by the state authority. 
Procedures for maintenance of the meter will be conducted in accordance with national 
procedures and standards.  
Applied methodology ACM0022 requires sampling for the baseline emissions calculation. 
Sampling take s place at the inlet of the feeding system to the reactors (mechanical conveyor 
system), to ensure the collected material is representative of the screened organic fraction. Each 
sampling event will include at least three grab samples taken at different times during the day 
(morning, midday, evening), with each grab sample weighing at least 10 kg, leading to a total 
sample weight of at least 30 kg per event.  Collected samples are homogenized and reduced 
using standard procedures such as the quartering method or coning & splitting to obtain a 5 –10 
kg laboratory sample. Each type of waste is separated and measured separately, to calculate 
their composition. 
Planning and Scheduling Internal Audits  
The quality assurance manager ( QAM) shall develop an annual internal audit schedule covering 
all relevant processes, including waste sorting, anaerobic digestion, landfill operations, biogas 
production, electricity generation, and associated measurement devices.  The schedule shall 
provide that all critical processes are audited at least once per year.  Auditors assigned shall be 
independent of the activities they audit to maintain objectivity.  
Conducting Internal Audits  
Auditors shall prepare an audit plan outlining the scope, objectives, criteria, and methods.  The 
audit shall include:  Review of process documentation and records (e.g., weighbridge records, 
waste characterization data, meter readings) ; interviews with personnel involved in the 
processes, and On-site observations of operations and equipment (e.g., biogas analyzer, 
flowmeters). Audit findings shall be based on objective evidence collected during the audit  
Identifying non-conformities 
Non-conformities shall be identified when deviations from procedures, standards, or regulatory 
requirements are observed . Each non-conformity shall be documented with:  a clear description 
of the issue, reference to the specific requirement or standard violated, and evidence supporting 
the finding (e.g., photographs, records.  
Reporting audit findings 
Auditors shall compile an internal audit report summarizing:  audit scope and objectives , areas 
audited, non-conformities identified , and o bservations and opportunities for improvement.  The 
report shall be submitted to the QAM and relevant department managers within five working days 
of the audit completion  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
151 
Addressing non-conformities 
Department managers shall review the non -conformities relevant to their areas.  A root cause 
analysis shall be performed for each non -conformity to identify underlying issues . A corrective 
action plan (CAP) shall be developed, including:  Actions to address immediate issues ; Steps to 
eliminate root causes; and responsibilities and timelines . The CAP shall be submitted to the QAM 
for approval within ten working days of receiving the audit report . 
Implementing Corrective Actions  
Department managers shall ensure corrective actions are implemented as per the approved CAP. 
Progress shall be monitored and documented.  Any delays or obstacles shall be communicated to 
the QAM promptly 
Verification and Closure  
Upon completion of corrective actions, the QAM or designated auditor shall verify the 
effectiveness of the actions taken.  verification m ust include one of the items : re-auditing the 
affected areas ; Reviewing updated records and documentation ; and confirming changes with 
personnel. If the corrective action is effective, the non -conformity shall be closed, and records 
updated. Ineffective corrective actions shall require a reassessment and development of 
additional actions . 
Documentation and Records  
All audit reports, non -conformity records, CAPs, and verification records shall be maintained by 
the QAM . Records shall be retained for a minimum of five years and be readily accessible for 
review. 
Continuous Improvement 
QAM shall analyze audit results periodically to identify trends and systemic issues. Findings shall 
be communicated to senior management for strategic decision-making. Lessons learned shall be 
integrated into training programs and procedure updates  
Calibration plans:  
The QA Manager establishes and maintains a Calibration Schedule for all relevant equipment.  
The schedule specifies equipment identification , calibration frequency, last calibration date, next 
calibration due date  and responsible person/entity . 
Calibration frequencies are determined based on manufacturer recommendations, regulatory 
requirements, and operational criticality.  
Calibration of all gas analyzers are performed by t he company MRU BACA GAZI LTD. It visits the 
project site and p erforms calibration every 6 months (manufacturer recommendation). G as 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
152 
analyzer device of the booster unit  measures the values of O 2 (%), H2S (ppm), CO 2 (%) and CH 4 
(%) are measured. Please see the calibration certificates documents.  
Flowmeter, pressure sensor and temperature sensors are calibrated every 5 -10 years according 
to manufacturer's specification . 
Weighbridge is calibration period is biannual.  
Electricity meters are under the jurisdiction of TEIAS and casually calibration is performed every 
10 years. Both the Biotrend company and TEIAS monitors the values measured by main and spare 
meters. If the reading is out of tolerance, TEIAS either performs calibration on-site all replace the 
meter with a calibrated meter. 
It's QA manager's duty to m aintain an up -to-date list of all monitoring equipment requiring 
calibration. 
Calibration Standards  
All calibration standards are traceable to national or international standards. They are performed 
by qualified and verified (by manufacturer) third party technicians on -site and off-site. Document 
resulted in the calibration record are kept by both the Biotrend company and responsible entity. 
This records also demonstrates any malfunctioning parts and can recommend device 
replacement if needed.  
Post Calibration 
Calibration Technicians shall review results to ensure instruments are within acceptable limits.  If 
an instrument is found out of tolerance,  QA Manager is immediately notified. Then previous 
measurements are investigated. C orrective actions as per the Non -Conformity Procedure  are 
performed. 
Emergencies Procedures  
The running hours of the power plants will be monitored as part of the monitoring procedures. In 
case of failure of one of the monitoring devices, portable instruments will be used in order to 
carry out periodic daily monitoring of the missing parameter(s) . These data are recorded on 
paper. Biotrend defined emergency procedures according to the provider recommendations.  
General procedure is as follow:  
Detection and Reporting : Upon detecting a meter failure or abnormal readings, the employee 
must immediately notify the Shift Supervisor.  If the failure poses an immediate safety risk (e.g., 
gas leaks), activate the emergency shutdown procedures and alarm systems.  
Initial Assessment: The Shift Supervisor shall assess the situation to determine the extent of the 
failure and potential impacts on safety and operations.  If necessary, isolate the affected area or 
equipment to prevent further risks.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
153 
Notification: The Shift Supervisor must inform the Operations Manager and Maintenance 
Manager immediately.  If regulatory reporting is required (e.g., environmental release), the 
Operations Manager will handle communications with authorities.  
Implementation of Contingency Measures : 
Activation of Backup Systems : If available, switch to backup meters or systems to maintain 
monitoring capabilities.  Verify the functionality and calibration of backup devices before use.  
Manual Monitoring : Implement manual data collection procedures using portable instruments.  
Assign qualified personnel to record readings at specified intervals.  
Operational Adjustments : Adjust operational parameters to safe levels based on available data.  
Reduce or suspend operations if safety or compliance cannot be assured.  
Repair and Restoration 
Fault Diagnosis: The maintenance manager shall assign technicians to diagnose the cause of the 
meter failure. Utilize diagnostic tools and consult equipment manuals as needed.  
Repair or Replacement: Repair the failed meter if feasible; otherwise, replace it with a calibrated 
unit. Ensure all repairs or replacements meet manufacturer specifications and regulatory 
requirements. 
Calibration and Testing : Calibrate the repaired or new meter according to the Calibration 
Procedure. Conduct functional tests to confirm accurate operation.  
Communication and Documentation  
Internal Communication : Provide regular updates to all relevant departments during the 
emergency. Debrief all staff once normal operations are restored.  
External Communication : If required, the Operations Manager will report the incident to 
regulatory bodies within the stipulated timeframe.  Prepare incident reports as per regulatory 
guidelines. 
Record Keeping : Document all actions taken during the emergency, including timelines, 
personnel involved, and decisions made.  File all records in accordance with the Document 
Control Procedure. 
Post-Emergency Review 
Incident Investigation : Conduct a root cause analysis to identify the underlying causes of the 
meter failure. Use appropriate methodologies (e.g., 5 Whys, Fault Tree Analysis).  
Corrective Actions : Develop a Corrective Action Plan to address identified issues.  Assign 
responsibilities and set deadlines for implementation.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
154 
Preventive Measures: Update maintenance schedules to prevent future occurrences. Review and 
revise operational procedures if necessary.  
Training: Conduct training sessions for staff on new procedures or changes.  Ensure all training is 
documented and filed appropriately.  
Safety Considerations 
Always prioritize personal and environmental safety over operational concerns.  Use appropriate 
Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) during all emergency and repair activities. Follow all 
lockout/tagout procedures when working on electrical or mechanical systems.  
Documentation 
Maintain records of the following  documents: Incident Reports , Repair and Maintenance Logs , 
Calibration Certificates , Communication Logs, Training Records, Root Cause Analysis Reports and 
Corrective and Preventive Action Plans .

7.2 Baseline Emissions  
Baseline emissions are determined as follows:  
ACM0001 Methodology 
Baseline emissions from the the SWDS ( BECH4,y) 
Baseline emissions scenario of the project is the methane from the open -air, SWDS, and the 
electricity supplied by the grid to be substituted, which will be calculated as follows according to 
the methodology ACM0001 V.19. 
It comprises the following sources:  
(a) Methane emissions from the SWDS in the absence of the project activity;  
(b) Electricity generation using fossil fuels or supplied by the grid in the absence of the project 
activity; 
(c) Heat generation using fossil fuels in the absence of the project activity; and  
(d) Natural gas used from the natural gas network in the absence of the project activity.  
𝐵𝐸𝑦 = 𝐵𝐸𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 + 𝐵𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 + 𝐵𝐸𝐻𝐺,𝑦 + 𝐵𝐸𝑁𝐺,𝑦                                                                  Equation (1) 
Where: 
BEy = Baseline emissions in year y (tCO 2e/yr) 
BECH4,y = Baseline emissions of methane from the SWDS in year y (tCO 2e/yr) 
BEEC,y = Baseline emissions associated with electricity generation in year y (tCO 2/yr) 
BEHG,y = Baseline emissions associated with heat generation in year y (tCO 2/yr) 
BENG,y = Baseline emissions associated with natural gas use in year y (tCO 2/yr)

Baseline emissions of methane from the SWDS are determined as follows, based on the amount

and destroyed in the baseline (such as due to regulations). In addition, the effect of methane 
oxidation that is present in the baseline and absent in the project is taken into accou nt: 
𝐵𝐸𝐶𝐻4 = ((1 − 𝑂𝑋𝑡𝑜𝑝_𝑙𝑎𝑦𝑒𝑟) × 𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝑃𝑗,𝑦 − 𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝐵𝐿,𝑦) × 𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 Equation (2)

𝑂𝑋𝑡𝑜𝑝_𝑙𝑎𝑦𝑒𝑟 = Fraction of methane in the LFG that would be oxidized in the top layer of the SWDS 
in the baseline (dimensionless)

𝐹 𝐶𝐻4,𝐸𝐿,𝑦 is determined using the “Tool to determine the mass flow of a greenhouse gas in a 
gaseous stream”. The following requirements apply:  
(e) As per the gaseous stream tool, if the LFG is used for multiple purposes (e.g. flaring or 
energy generation), and all methane destruction devices are verified to be operational (e.g. by 
means of flame detectors records, energy generated), a single flow  meter may be used to 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
164 
record the flow into multiple destruction devices. The destruction efficiency of the least 
efficient among the destruction devices shall be used as the destruction efficiency for all 
destruction devices monitored by this flow meter. If there are any period s for which one or more 
destruction devices are not operational, paragraph 5 (a) and (b) of the Appendix of the "Tool to 
determine the mass flow of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream" tool shall be followed;  
(f) CH4 is the greenhouse gas for which the mass flow should be determined;  
(g) The simplification offered for calculating the molecular mass of the gaseous stream is valid 
(equations (3) or (17) in the tool);  
(h) The mass flow should be calculated on an hourly basis for each hour h in year y;  
(i) The mass flow calculated for hour h is 0 if the equipment is not working in hour h (Opj,h=not 
working), the hourly values are then summed to a yearly unit basis.  
Amount of the methane used for power generation ( 𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝐸𝐿,𝑦) The “Tool to determine the mass 
of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream” provides following 6 options for measuring mass 
flow of a greenhouse gas i in a gaseous stream Table.  
Option Flow gaseous stream Volumetric Fraction 
A Volume flow – dry basis Dry or wet basis 
B Volume flow – wet basis Dry basis 
C Volume flow – wet basis Wet basis 
D Mass flow – dry basis Dry or wet basis  
E Mass flow – wet basis Dry basis 
F Mass flow – wet basis Wet basis 
 
Under project activity, Biogas flow measurements are converted Nm 3 by the flow meter79 
(corrected volume flow, page 9 of document) which use data from connected to the 
temperature and pressure sensors. Thus, measured values are automatically converted to 
normal conditions (0 °C and 101,325 Pa). According to methane density document of CDM 80, 
0.716 kg/m3 is the density at normal conditions. Further applying Option A from TOOL08 would 
result to overcounting baseline emissions.  
Thus biogas density value of 0.716 kg/m 3 (normal conditions) is taken from TOOL08 “Tool to 
determine the mass of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream”.

Baseline emissions associated with electricity generation (BE EC,y) 
The baseline emissions associated with electricity generation in year y (BE EC,y) shall be 
calculated using the "Methodological tool: Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions from 
electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity generation". When applying the tool:  
(a) The electricity sources k in the tool correspond to the sources of electricity generated 
identified in the selection of the most plausible baseline scenario; and  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
166 
(b) ECBL,k,y in the tool is equivalent to the net amount of electricity generated using LFG in year y 
(EGPJ,y). 
Taking into account the approach provided by the tool, baseline emissions are then calculated 
using the generic approach based on the quantity of electricity dispatched into the National 
Grid, an emission factor for electricity generation and a factor to account for transmission 
losses, as follows: 
𝐵𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 = 𝛴𝐸𝐶𝐵𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 × 𝐸𝐹𝐸𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 × (1 + 𝑇𝐷𝐿𝑘,𝑦) Equation (2) 
 
Where; 
𝐸𝐶𝐵𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 = Net amount of electricity generated using LFG in year y (MWh/yr)  
𝐸𝐹𝐸𝐿,𝑘,𝑦 = Emission factor for electricity generation for source k in year y (t CO 2/MWh) 
𝑇𝐷𝐿𝑘,𝑦 = Average technical transmission and distribution losses for providing electricity to source 
k in year y 
k = Sources of electricity generated in the baseline  
The latest Emission Factor is given from Turkish National Electricity Network calculated annually 
and for the year 2020 the combined emission factor is 0 .5410 tCO2/MWh and. The average 
technical transmission and distribution losses factor is 9.9703 for year 2020, 9.630 0% for year 
2021, 9.1018% for year 2022 and 9.2181% for year 2023. 
BEEC,y = 1,739.805 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.099703) = 1,035 tCO2e (year 2020, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 21,201.337 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.096300) = 12,574 tCO2e (year 2021, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 40,625.781 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.091018) = 23,978 tCO2e (year 2022, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 4,142.851 x 0.5410 x (1 + 0.092181) = 2,447 tCO2e (year 2023, rounded-down) 
BEEC,y = 1,035 + 12,574 + 23,978 + 2,447 = 40,034

According to appendix of TOOL08, data substitution is possible to calculate methane mass flow.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
172 
If missing data are encountered in the course of determining the methane mass flow, it  may be 
substituted with conservative data sets (see below) from specific periods.  However, data 
substitution shall only be applied to either the methane concentration or  the biogas volumetric  
flow readings, but not to both simultaneously. If data is missing for  both parameters during a 
given period of time, no data substitution shall be allowed for  that period. 
Substitution as outlined in table below may be undertaken only if the following  conditions are 
met: 
(a) For methane concentration, biogas flow rates during the period where data gap occurred (data 
gap period) shall be consistent with normal operation (i.e. the  average flow rates during the gap 
period shall not deviate from the average flow rates of the period taken for data substitution (data 
substitution period) by more than +/ - 20%); and 
(b) For biogas flow rate, methane concentration during the data gap period shall be  consistent 
with the methane concentration observed during normal operations (i.e.  the average methane 
concentration during the data gap period shall not deviate  from the average methane 
concentration of the data substitution period by more  than +/- 20%); and 
(c) Project participants shall demonstrate that the methane is being destroyed during  the period 
of the data gap. If corroborating parameters fail to demonstrate any of  these requirements, no 
substitution shall be allowed.  
 
By following outlined rules, a data substitution procedure is utilized to realize achieved GHG 
emissions.  
Duration of Missing Data  Data Substitution procedure  
Less than six hours  Use the weighted average of the four hours 
period immediately before and four hours 
period immediately after the outage  
Six to 24 hours Use the upper bound or lower bound of 95% 
confidence interval of the data spanning 24 
hours prior to and 24 hours after the outage, 
whichever results in more conservative 
estimate of emission reductions  
One to seven days Use the upper bound or lower bound of 95% 
confidence 
Greater than one week interval of the data spanning 72 hours prior to 
and 72 hours

𝐿𝐸𝑦 = 𝐿𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀𝑃,𝑦 + 𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 + 𝐿𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 Equation (4) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝑦 = Leakage emissions in the year  y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀𝑃,𝑦 = Leakage emissions from composting or co -composting in year  y 
(t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = Leakage emissions from anaerobic digester in year  y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB in year  y (t CO2e) 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
180 
 
Since there is no composting activity under project activity, Leakage emissions associated with 
composting ( LECOMP,y) is considered zero”. LE AD,y and LE RDF_SB,y are considered for the project 
activity. 
Leakage emissions from anaerobic digestion (LE AD,y) 
Leakage emissions associated with the anaerobic digester ( 𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦) depend on how the digestate 
is managed. They include emissions associated with storage and composting of the digestate 
and are determined as follows:  
𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦  =  𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦  + 𝐿𝐸𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑝,𝑦 Equation (5) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = Leakage emissions from anaerobic digester in year  y (tCO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑝,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with composting digestate in year 
y (tCO2e) 
LEcomp,y is not considered due to composting being part of project activity. Leakage emissions 
associated with storage of digestate is accounted under project activity.  
Determining LEstorage,y for liquid digestate 
Option 2: Procedure using a default value  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = Fww,CH4,default  x QCH4,𝑦 x GWPCH4 Equation (7) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
Fww,CH4,default = 
Default factor representing the remaining methane production 
capacity of liquid digestate (fraction)  
QCH4,𝑦  = Quantity of methane produced in the digester in year y (tCH 4) 
𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (28 tCO2 / tCH4) 
 
According to TOOL14, Fww,CH4,default is selected based on the technology of anerobic digesters. 
Under project activity, plug flow reactors under thermophilic conditions are utilized. Due to high 
COD removal efficiency of such reactors, minimum default value of "0.05" is selected.  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 0.05  x 5,109.838 x 28 = 7,153.773 tCO2e  
 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
181 
Determining LE storage,y for solid digestate 
Option 2: Procedure using default values  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = FSD,CH4,default  x QCH4,𝑦 x GWPCH4 Equation (8) 
 
Where: 
 
 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
Fww,CH4,default = 
Default factor representing the remaining methane production 
capacity of liquid digestate (fraction)  
QCH4,𝑦  = Quantity of methane produced in the digester in year y (tCH 4) 
𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (tCO2 / tCH4) 
 
According to TOOL14, F ww,CH4,default is selected based on the technology of anerobic digesters. 
Under project activity, plug flow reactors under thermophilic conditions are utilized. Due to high 
COD removal efficiency of such reactors, minimum default value of "0.05" is selected.  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 0.15  x 5,109.938 x 28=21,461.32 tCO2e

7.5 Net GHG Emission Reductions and Removals 
 
To calculate the emission reductions the project participant shall apply the following equation:  
𝐸𝑅𝑦 = 𝐵𝐸𝑦 − 𝑃𝐸𝑦 − 𝐿𝐸𝑦 Equation (5)

𝐿𝐸𝑦 = Leakage emissions in year  y (t CO2e)

Justification for the difference  
137,692 
152,852 11.010% 1. Currently, landfill gas is being generated from two 
lots. Lot-1 is full but remains active, while waste 
disposal continues in Lot -2. Waste deposition in 
Lot-1 began in 2013. Although the project has 
been a sanitary landfill from the start, the current 
site management is more efficient, with a greater 
focus on landfill gas collection through improved 
cover and gas collection systems, leading to 
better landfill gas collection. The historical waste 
projection is based on a population model, which 
inherently carries uncertainty regarding both the 
disposed waste volume and its composition. Ex -
ante baseline emissions from the SWDS are 
estimated at 94 ,739 tCO₂e, while ex-post 
emission reduction for the same category 
amounts to 135,140 tCO ₂e. While this difference 
may seem significant, it can be attributed to 
inherent uncertainties in the projection model  
made with best available data . Also as stated 
below, biomethanization plant’s commissioning is 
delayed by eight months and during this time  all 
MSW is disposed to landfill.  
2. "According to the applied methodology ACM0022 
Version 03.0, Paragraph 33, ‘Baseline methane 
emissions from the SWDS are determined using 
“TOOL04: Emissions from solid waste disposal 
sites.”’ The TOOL04 model estimates the amount 
of methane that would have been generated 
under baseline conditions. This represen ts a 
conservative approach, as baseline emission 
credit generation accelerates over time. The 
introduction of the biomethanization facility was 
delayed until mid -2021, and waste records for 
July 2021 were unavailable. As a result, only 
10,363 tCO ₂e (rounded down) out of the 
expected 40,362 tCO₂e was achieved during the 
first crediting period. Due to this delay, organic 
waste was disposed of in the landfill for eight 
months after electricity generation began."  
3. Net electricity generation is achieved as 
67,709.774 MWh while estimation was 
73,637.783 MWh. The difference of 8.050% is 
within the acceptable margin of error.   
4. During the two -year pandemic period (2019 –
2021), global stay -at-home measures led to an 
increase in solid waste with higher food content 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
185 
being disposed of. This resulted in enhanced gas 
generation from landfills prior to the introduction 
of the biomethanization facility .83

1 PROJECT DETAILS

text_blocks.heading: 1.1 Summary Description of the Project
text_blocks.text:
Bergama integrated solid waste management facility and electricity generation facility  is a waste 
handling and disposal project where Municipal Solid Wastes (MSW) from several districts of Izmir 
province is disposed to Bergama Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility , mechanically 
separated and utilized for power generation. Upon the acceptance of mixed MSW, waste streams 
are separated based on type at the Mechanical Separation Facility and, the biodegradable wastes 
are sent to  biomethanization Facility . The remaining fractions are sent to the existing sanitary 
landfill and recovered recyclable wastes is sent to licensed companies. The biogas generated 
from the biomethanization facility, and the generated LFG is converted into electrical energy by 
gas engines then supplied to the Türkiye’s national grid thus generates revenue. Liquid and 
fermented products are found polluted by project owner due to poor waste separation at source  
public practice therefore cannot be utilized as intended. Solid fraction is sent to landfill and liquid 
fraction is treated as leachate. Due to project activity, the waste is stabilized, the amount of waste 
to be stored is minimized, electricity  generation is  utilized, and the negative environmental 
effects of wastes are eliminated. RDF production is also planned and to be implemented after 
January 2024. It will be used for electricity generation in  Cine Biomass Power Plant and Ezine 
Biomass Power Plant which are operated by subsidiary company of İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik 
Üretim A.Ş. 
Electricity generation capacity (licence capacity) of project is 9.898 MWe (10.157 MWm) and 
project, landfill are located in Izmir Province, Bergama District, Kaşıkçı Neighborhood . Project is 
owned and operated by İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik Üretim A.Ş.   
Prior to project implementation, MSW was disposed of to sanitary landfill where generated LFG 
was vented to atmosphere. Project activity contributed to following goals  
• Reduction in fossil fuel use (imported or local) by using renewable energy resources  
• Displacement of ecologically unsound and climate unfriendly power generation  
• Extension of nationally sourced power generation  
• Improvement of environmental conditions (GHG and odour) and safety in the landfill area  
• Reduce the greenhouse gas emissions  from being emitted directly to the atmosphere from 
waste at the Bergama SWDS that would be otherwise left to be decomposed. , 
Over the projected 7 -year crediting period, the project is estimated to achieve a net emission 
reduction of 1,378,074 tCO2e (metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent) and 196,867 tCO2e as 
annual average. Estimated gross electricity generation is expected to reach full capacity, at year 
2024, which is 74,235 MWh/year and cumulative gross electricity generation  estimation for the 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
5 
first crediting period  is 424,247.80 MWh. The starting date of the project activity is 31-October-
2020, which is the provisional acceptance date of the first two gas engines.  
In the first monitoring period , which is between 31 -October-2020 and 31 -January-2023 (both 
dates are included) , 67,709.774 MWh of net electricity is generated and 152,852 tCO2e 
emission reduction is achieved.

text_blocks.heading: 1.8 Project Start Date
text_blocks.text:
According to Verra standard v4.7 section 3.8, "The project start date of a non -AFOLU project is 
the date on which the project began generating GHG emission reductions or carbon dioxide 
removals". Before the project activity there were neither energy genera tion nor flaring system 
installed on -site. Project start date is 31-October-2020, which corresponds with  the provisional 
acceptance of first and second gas engines where continuous operation started. Flare equipment 
is part of the booster unit and designed  to be used during emergency situations. Flare was 
installed on -site on 07-October-2020 and tested by the manufacturer on -site before the 
commissioning of gas engines. Between equipment tests and gas engine, flare equipment was 
not operational.

text_blocks.heading: 1.11 Description of the Project Activity
text_blocks.text:
The project activity includes waste reception area, mechanical separation unit, sanitary landfill, 
biomethanization facility (anaerobic reactors)  and LFG to power plant. 
Mixed municipal wastes collected from the project area will be brought to the waste reception 
unit of the Facility and after all of them enter the Mechanical Separation Facility, they will be 
separated as biodegradable waste, residual waste, and waste suitable for recycling.  
Majority of the biodegradable waste are sent to the Biomethanization Facility, and residual 
biodegradable wastes that cannot be recovered  are sent to the Sanitary Landfill Area along with 
inert fractions. Project is within the scope of the Temporary Article 1 of the Regulation on Regular 
Storage of Wastes, which was published in the Official Gazette dated 26 -March-2010 and 
numbered 27533.  
Portion of the recycled wastes that are suitable for recovery is sent to licensed disposal facilities 
and contribute to the circular economy. Remaining waste  fraction is sent to the Landfill for 
disposal. 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
10 
 
Figure 1. Process Flow Diagram of Bergama Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility  
The capacity of the Mechanical Separation Facility to be established within the scope of the 
project has been determined as 435 tons/day  initially, taking into account the total waste 
generation amount to be generated according to the population of Bergama and Dikili district in 
the target year 2035. The units to be found in the system 3 are given below. It will be a fully 
automatic system without manual separation.  
• Waste Reception Area (part of mechanical separation building)  
• Bag Opener 
• Conveyors 
• Trommel Sieve 
• Magnetic Separator 
• Optical Separator 
• Ballistic Separator 
 
3 Details of the system is taken from PO and the EIA report. Kindly refer to latest EIA report: 
http://eced.csb.gov.tr/jsp/ek1/47147  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
11 
• Bale Press 
• Product/Waste Accumulation Area (The area where the waste bales pressed in the bale press 
machine are stored) 
• Product Warehouse (Biodegradable wastes and residual wastes will be collected in closed 
trailers that will be constantly present)  
The biodegradable fraction of MSW separated  from the Mechanical Separation Facility is utilized 
by Biomethanization Facility, where 27.30%  is converted to  solid fermented product, 36.35%  is 
converted to liquid fermented product and 36.35% is decomposition loss  (biogas). 
A part of the liquid fermented product formed in the Biomethanization Facility is recycled back to 
the system  and, the remaining portion is se nt to the balancing pool . In order to meet the 
conditions for the discharge of water into the sewer system in accordance with the provisions of 
the Water Pollution Control Regulation and the IZSU Wastewater Discharge into the Sewage 
Network, liquid fermented products along with leachate is sent to leachate treatment facility .  
Approximately 75% of the liquid fermented product is recycled back to the system. The remaining 
25% is transferred to the treatment plant to be established within the scope of the project.  
The solid fermented product formed in the Biomethanization Facility is directed to the landfill due 
to fact it doesn’t satisfy criteria to be used as fertilizer. Non-standard product are disposed to the 
existing Landfill. 
The Biomethanization Facility ha d an optimal capacity of 450 tons/day, where around 300 tons 
of biodegradable waste is utilized for year 2022.  The final capacity of the Biomethanization 
Facility will be reached by the upcoming  years. Maximum capacity of 180 tons/day (3.512 
m3/reactor) are built in the Biomethanization Facility.  
The units to be found in the system are given below.  
• Anaerobic Reactors 
• Dewatering Unit and Liquid Fermented Product Storage  
• Solid Fermented Product Warehouse  
• Mixing System 
• Gas Storage Unit 
• Gas Purification System  
• Gas Engines 
• Booster Unit (with Flare)

The biogas generated in the Biomethanization Facility and the methane gas that will be drawn 
from the Landfill Gas collection system from the Landfill Area will be converted into electricity 
and heat in the Electricity Generation Facility to be established. All of the electrical ener gy to be 
obtained will be transferred to the interconnected system and economic gain will be achieved. 
The heat will be used in the Biomethanization Plant process.  
Heat and electrical energy will be obtained by burning the methane gas to be generated in the 
landfill lots currently being used in the Electricity Generation Facility to be established within the 
scope of the project and the biogas to be formed in the Bio methanization Facility to be added 
within the scope of this project.  
The capacity of the Electricity Generation Facilities to be established within the scope of the 
project has been determined as 9.898 MWe. According to the generation licence, there are seven 
gas engines with a capacity of 1.414 MWe will be implemented in the facility. As of 30-September-
2022, 6 gas engines are operational . 7th gas engine is planned to be installed in 2024 . LFG to 
power generation units are given below.

text_blocks.heading: 1.13 Conditions Prior to Project Initiation
text_blocks.text:
Within the borders of İzmir Province, Bergama District, Kaşıkçı Neighborhood, Bergama 
Municipality took the decision of "Environmental Impacts are Insignificant" with the decision 
number 162/2002/02 and dated 28 -February-2002 and started to be operated by Bergama 
Municipality as "Bergama Regular Solid Waste Disposal Facility" in 2011. The facility continued 
to be operated by the Izmir Metropolitan Municipality as of 01 -October-2014 with the law 
numbered 6360. 
Prior to the proposed project activity, the wastes were left to decay within landfill site and 
methane was emitted to the atmosphere without any control or utilization.  
Please refer to Section 3.4 for further details on baseline scenario .

text_blocks.heading: 1.16.3 Supply Chain (Scope 3) Emissions
text_blocks.text:
In this project, emissions are reduced from the electricity sector in TÜRKİYE by implementing 
renewable energy sources and energy efficiency measures. The produced good in this project is 
electricity, which falls under Scope 1 and 2 emissions of industrial  producers. As such, there is 
no direct involvement of any specific producer or retailer of the impacted good or service in this 
project. 
Double counting is a common source of concern in the tracking and reporting of emissions 
reductions because it can result in overstated emissions reductions, misrepresentation of actual 
emissions, and undermine the integrity of the emissions reduction cert ification process. To 
address this concern, it is vital to have a robust and transparent tracking and reporting 
mechanism in place, which is exactly what we have done with our LFG project.  
TÜRKİYE has a centralized national grid system, which means that all the electricity generated 
by the project is fed into the same grid and distributed to all consumers. This is a crucial factor 
in avoiding the risk of double counting, as the electricity g enerated from our project is not 
separately metered and credited, but instead blended with other sources of electricity in the grid, 
so there is no way to determine the exact proportion of electricity generated from our project.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
22

text_blocks.heading: 1.17.1 Sustainable Development Contributions Activity Description
text_blocks.text:
The Project will contribute to sustainable development in the following ways:  
• Reduce CH 4 emission. The project will recover and destroy landfill gas that consists 
mainly of greenhouse gas methane and would otherwise be released directly into the 
atmosphere, effectively reducing greenhouse gas emissions, which contributes to the 
Turkey’s commitment to carbon dioxide emissions;  
• Increase electricity production from renewable sources, which will certainly reduce the 
consumption of fossil fuel in Turkish Power Grid and further help to achieve the national 
action to promote renewable energy development;  
• Improve the operational safety of the landfills. The project reduces the potential safety 
hazards of landfill gas explosion by recycling landfill gas and improves the safety of 
landfill operation;  
• The project will also provide new jobs opportunities and increase tax revenue, which will 
have a positive effect on the local economy.  
• Improving air quality (i.e. by reducing odor) and therefore having positive effects on the 
local environment.  
Nevertheless, it is still possible to note that the project will make positive contributions to at least 
six Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). These are:  
SDG Goal 5: Gender equality : Target 5.5, Ensure women’s full and effective participation and 
equal opportunities for leadership at all levels of decision making in political, economic and 
public life.  Sub indicator 5.5.2 description is “ Proportion of women in managerial positions ”. 
There is an initiative called “30 Percent Club” to promote women participation in the boards and 
top executive teams internationally and domestically.  Türkiye’s 30 Percent Club initiative’s aims 
are women participation in the boards and top executive teams of BIST Star Market, Main Market 
companies and BIST 100 companies 5. Biotrend company has joined the initiative 6 and number 
of women managers on board satisfy 30% condition 78. KPIs (5.5.1) to track the projects’ 
contribution to SDG 5 is number of women in managerial positions to total number of managerial

positions. There is no appropriate value is find for this SDG indicator in Türkiye, and voluntary 
contribution to 30 percent club and satisfying the condition should satisfy the KPI 5.5.1.  
SDG Goal 7: Affordable and Clean Energy:  Target 7.2: Increase global percentage of renewable 
energy. Sub indicator 7.2.1 description is “ Renewable energy share in the total final energy 
consumption”. The proposed Project is a waste to power project that will generate renewable 
energy by capturing methane from municipal waste and utilizing it to produce thermal and electric 
energy through gas engine systems. By supplying renewable energy generated at th e plant to the 
national grid, the proposed Project will contribute to increasing the share of renewable energy in 
the global energy mix and the proportion of the population with  primary reliance on clean fuels 
and technology. KPIs (7.2.1) to track the projects’ contribution to SDG 7 is  amount of renewable 
energy supplied to grid for consumption.   
SDG Goal 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth : Target 8.5: Full employment and decent work 
with equal pay. The demand for food and electric energy is rapidly increasing in Turkey for various 
reasons, such as industrialization, urbanization, economic development, and population growth. 
The country's external dependence on agricultural products has intensified be cause the 
increasing demand cannot be met by a decreasing domestic agricultural production capacity 
which is due to several reasons, such as shrinkage  of agricultural land, increasing migration to 
urban spaces from rural areas where agricultural production is densely located, and dramatic 
increase in the cost of inputs for agricultural production. Therewithal, Turkey, which cannot meet 
its increasing el ectricity demand due to the deprivation of conventional resources used to 
generate energy, such as coal, oil, and natural gas, has also become foreign -dependent on 
energy. Through its implementation, the proposed project activity will contribute to reducin g 
Turkey's foreign dependency by generating renewable energy out of municipal wastes. In addition, 
as an LFG- based renewable energy technology implementation, the proposed project activity will 
achieve higher levels of economic productivity; hence, increa sing the annual growth rate of real 
GDP per employed person. Moreover, it will increase the region's employment capacity while 
decreasing the unemployment rate. KPIs (8.5.2) to track the projects’ contribution to SDG 8 via 
target 8.5 is  average earning of females and male employees engaged in the project and 
segregated by age and persons with disabilities.  According to 11th development plan of Türkiye9, 
women employment rate is 38.5 and women presentation in the parliament is 20%. Thus 
baseline is 38.5%.  
SDG Goal 10: Reduce inequality within and among countries: Target 10.4: Adopt policies, 
especially fiscal, wage and social protection policies, and progressively achieve greater equality . 
Sub indicator 10.4.1 description is “Labour share of GDP”.  Corporate governance 10 ensures

SDG Goal 13: Climate Action: Target 13.2: Integrate climate change measures into national 
policies, strategies, and planning. Sub indicator 13.2.2 description is “ Total greenhouse gas 
emissions per year ”. The proposed project activity will reduce GHG emissions by capturing and 
utilizing methane, one of the most potent GHGs triggering climate change.  It is estimated that

SDG Goal 17: Partnership for Goals: Target 17.3: Mobilize additional financial resources for 
developing countries from multiple sources. Sub indicator 17.3.1 description is “ Additional

of foreign direct investment . According to a report of Istanbul Chamber of Commerce  on foreign

is taken as baseline.

text_blocks.heading: 1.18 Additional Information Relevant to the Project
text_blocks.text:
Leakage Management 
Leakage emissions are considered as per TOOL14: Project and leakage emissions from 
anaerobic digesters. Default value is adopted for pro ject emissions from biogas leak.  
Under project activity, LFG generated from landfill goes directly to gas engines where flare system 
can destroy excess gas during emergency situations.  There are multiple pressure sensors with 
digester and at the booster that can detect leaks based on unusual pressure drops. When such 
conditions occur, leak location is found by plant technicians and bypass lines continue operation. 
HDPE pipes allow leak repairs and gas balloon lets extra down-time for repairs. Under worst case 
where such measures cannot be applied for  generated biogas , safety valves can be used for 
venting extra biogas and electricity production would be halted. No such scenario realized before 
and plant operation is constantly monitored for maximum performance and leak prevention.  
Commercially Sensitive Information  
No commercially sensitive information has been excluded from the public version of the project 
description. 
Further Information 
There is no further information to be shared.  
2 SAFEGUARDS

text_blocks.heading: 2.1 No Net Harm
text_blocks.text:
The Ministry of Environment and Urbanization has authorized the Environmental Impact 
Assessment (EIA) Report. According to Turkish rules and regulations, an EIA's permission may 
only be granted if the project in question has no detrimental effects on the e nvironment or the 
economy, thus the project has no net harm. All kinds of preventive measures will be taken to 
minimize the odor, dust, leachate, gas and similar negative effects that may arise from the facility. 
These preventive measures are explained in Environmental Impact Assessment Report in further 
detail. The landfill floor will be constructed in accordance with the provisions of the "Regulation 
on the Landfilling of Wastes" and the Circular on the Preparation of the Sanitary Landfills 
Implementation Project, numbered 2018/15, which was published in the Official Gazette dated 
26-March-2010 and numbered 27533. A letter of conformity will be obtained from the relevant 
institution regarding the impermeability of the sealed floors and the impermeable pool s 
throughout the project. If it is determined that the springs, fountains, groundwaters and 
boreholes in the vicinity of the activity area are adversely affected in terms of water quantity and 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
30 
quality, the activity will be stopped immediately, and the project will be terminated, and any 
damage will be borne by the activity owner.

text_blocks.heading: 2.2 Local Stakeholder Consultation
text_blocks.text:
Within the scope of the Environmental Impact Assessment Regulation, which was published in 
the Official Gazette dated 25 -November-2014 and numbered 29186, a public participation 
meeting was held at the place and date determined by the Provincial Directorate of Environment 
and Urbanization in order to inform the public about the investment and to receive their opinions 
and suggestions regarding the project. The Public Participation Meeting was held on 12 -
December-2019, under the presidency of İzmir Provincial Directorate of Environment and 
Urbanization, in Sindel District, District Coffeehouse. Please note that the photo of the meeting 
is given below. 
 
Figure 5. Public Participation Meeting  
Before the meeting date, the meeting was announced in both local and regional newspapers. The 
newspaper announcements are given in the appendix.  
During the Public Participation Meeting, the environmental impacts of the Project were explained 
by the company representative who prepared the EIA report and the questions were answered. 
Information about the project was given with a visual presentation w hich included the 
environmental impacts that will occur during the construction and operation phases and the 
precautions to be taken.  
There is a system established by İzmir Novtek Enerji Elektrik Üretim A. Ş. for maintaining 
communication with internal and external shareholders. External shareholders can reach the 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
31 
company via website or phone numbers and use default templates for this specific mechanism. 
There is also a specific box for collecting complaints, suggestions, or comments in on-site offices. 
Logbooks will be used for continuous feedback from local stakeholders. A logbook was left to the 
Mukhtar where complaints and wishes can be conveyed. The Mukhtar will be visited and checked 
regularly. 
Plant managers are directly responsible for keeping communication with locals directly or through 
Mukhtars. The Human  resources department are responsible for communication with internal 
shareholders and corporate communication department is responsible for communicating with 
external shareholders.

text_blocks.heading: 2.3 Environmental Impact
text_blocks.text:
An environmental impact assessment dated in 20 -August-2020 was prepared to assess the 
environmental effects of the project activity. The report has been prepared in accordance with 
Turkey’s national standards. Therefore, the project is implemented according to the national laws 
and regulations if the environm ental precautions stated in the report are applied. EIA has been 
conducted to assess all potential impacts and minimize through measures adopted.  
A public participation meeting was held on 12 -December-2019, within the scope of the 
Environmental Impact Assessment Regulation, which was published in the Official Gazette dated 
25-November-2014 and numbered 29186. The meeting’s purpose was to inform the public about 
the investment and to receive their opinions and suggestions regarding the project.  
A capacity increase, implementation of Refuse Drive Fuel production and exclusion of composting 
is described in EIA update which is undergoing bureaucratic review and can be reached with a 
search in official website 24. 
Environmental impact  assessment report is accepted on 11 -September-2020 with decision 
number of 53785661-220.01-E.191230.

text_blocks.heading: 2.4 Public Comments
text_blocks.text:
There was great interest to the Public Participation Meeting held regarding the project. The 
meeting was started by İzmir Provincial Directorate of Environment and Urbanization EIA Branch 
Manager S. Deniz SEVİMOĞLU. During the meeting, the presentation was made by the EIA Report 
Expert regarding the characteristics and environmental effects of the project was completed, and 
the public's opinions and suggestions were received after the presentation.  
During the meeting;  
• It was asked whether if similar facilities exist elsewhere in Turkey, and it was informed 
that the largest facility in this regard is in Ankara, Istanbul and Antalya.

text_blocks.heading: 24 https://eced -duyuru.csb.gov.tr/eced -prod/duyurular.xhtml
text_blocks.text:
Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
32 
• It has been said that there is no such thing as clean energy and that somehow the 
environment is harmed; Regarding this issue, it has been stated that no waste will be 
incinerated within the scope of the project, energy will be obtained from the methane gas 
that will be generated and there will be no environmental impact like the incineration 
plants. 
• It was asked whether there is any data on how much methane gas will be generated in 
the facility, and it was stated that methane gas that will be generated during the storage 
of garbage will be combusted, thus preventing the release of methane gas to the 
atmosphere. 
• It has been stated that the local people have concerns about the formation of an odor 
originating from the facilities, and it has been stated that the facilities will be planned in 
a completely closed manner, they will be made much better than the current situation 
and all necessary precautions will be taken.  
The meeting was ended without any problems by the İzmir Directorate of Environment and 
Urbanization and the meeting was recorded in the minutes.  
The project was open for public comment from 16-January-2023 to 15-February-2023.25 No 
comments have been made during the public comment period.

Applicability of ACM0001: Flaring or use of landfill gas --- Version 19.0 
Applicability Condition  Justification 
Paragraph 2 states (Scope) : 
This methodology applies to project activities 
that include the destruction of methane 
emissions and displacement of a more -GHG-
intensive service by capturing landfill gas 
from the landfill site and/or flaring and/or 
using to produce energy (i.e. electrici ty, 
thermal energy); and/or using to supply 
consumers through natural gas distribution 
network, dedicated pipeline or trucks  
Paragraph 2:  
Applicable. Project activity is the destruction 
of methane emissions and displacement of a 
more-GHG-intensive service by capturing 
landfill gas from the landfill site and 
generating electricity. 
Paragraph 3 states: 
The methodology is applicable under the 
following conditions: 
(a) Install a new LFG capture system in 
an existing or new (Greenfield) SWDS 
where no LFG capture system was or 
would have been installed prior to the 
implementation of the project activity; 
or 
Paragraph 3:  
a) Prior to the project activity, there was 
a vent system that allows LFG to be vented to 
the atmosphere. LFG capture and utilization 
system was implemented with the project 
activity. Thus, this criterion is applicable.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
39 
(b) Make an investment into an existing 
LFG capture system to increase the 
recovery rate or change the use of the 
captured LFG, provided that:  
(i) The captured LFG was vented or 
flared and not used prior to the 
implementation of the project activity; 
and 
(ii) In the case of an existing active LFG 
capture system for which the amount 
of LFG cannot be collected separately 
from the project system after the 
implementation of the project activity 
and its efficiency is not impacted on 
by the project system: historical  data 
on the amount of LFG capture and 
flared is available; 
(c) Flare the LFG and/or use the 
captured LFG in any (combination) of 
the following ways: 
(i) Generating electricity;  
(ii) Generating heat in a boiler, air heater 
or kiln (brick firing only) or glass 
melting furnace; and/or  
(iii) Supplying the LFG to consumers 
through a natural gas distribution 
network; 
(iv) Supplying compressed/liquefied LFG 
to consumers using trucks;  
(v) Supplying the LFG to consumers 
through a dedicated pipeline;  
Do not reduce the amount of organic waste 
that would be recycled in the absence of the 
project activity. 
b) i) Captured LFG was vented to the 
atmosphere before the implementation of 
project activity. 
Thus, this criterion is applicable.  
Criterion (ii) does not apply because there 
was no existing active LFG capture system 
prior to the implementation of the project 
activity.  
c) Captured LFG is used for generating 
electricity. Thus, criteria (i) is applicable. 
Criteria (ii), (iii), (iv) and (v) are not 
applicable. Flaring system is a part of 
booster units and designed to be operational 
only during emergencies such as failure of 
gas engines for extensive period of time.  
d) The organic waste that was brought 
to the site was sent to landfill and was not 
used for any purpose. As such there was no 
diversion of organic waste prior to project 
implementation. Furthermore, no diversion of 
organic waste has occurred during the 
project implementation. Thus, this criterion is 
applicable. 
Paragraph 4 states:  
(a) Atmospheric release of the LFG or 
capture of LFG and destruction through 
flaring to comply with regulations or 
contractual requirements, to address safety 
and odour concerns, or for other reasons; 
and  
Paragraph 4: 
(a) Applicable. The most feasible baseline 
scenario of the project is (a): Atmospheric 
release of the LFG; and for electricity 
generation: that electricity would be 
generated in the grid. Regulation on 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
40 
(b) In the case that the LFG is used in the 
project activity for generating electricity 
and/or generating heat in a boiler, air heater, 
glass melting furnace or kiln: (i) For 
electricity generation: that electricity would 
be generated in the grid or in cap tive fossil 
fuel fired power plants; and/or (ii) For heat 
generation: that heat would be generated 
using fossil fuels in equipment located within 
the project boundary;  
(c) In the case of LFG supplied to the end -
user(s) through natural gas distribution 
network, trucks or the dedicated pipeline, the 
baseline scenario is assumed to be 
displacement of natural gas.  
(d) In the case of LFG from a Greenfield 
SWDS, the identified baseline scenario is 
atmospheric release of the LFG or capture of 
LFG in a managed SWDS and destruction 
through flaring to comply with regulations or 
contractual requirements, to address safety 
and odour concerns, or for other reasons.  
Landfilling of Wastes , addresses landfill 
conditions, safety and odour concerns.  
(b) The electricity generated from the project 
activity is supplied to the grid. The generated 
electricity displaces electricity generated by 
the fossil fuel fired power plants in the grid. 
The project therefore complies with these 
criteria (i). Criterion is applicable. (ii) does not 
apply because the project does not generate 
heat. 
(c) This criterion is not applicable. The project 
does not supply LFG to the end user(s) 
through a natural gas distribution network, 
trucks or dedicated pipeline.  
(d) This applicability criterion does not apply 
to the project activity instance because the 
LFG arises from existing SWDSs. 
Furthermore, there are no requirements to 
capture LFG or destroy LFG through flaring in 
order to comply with regulations or 
contractual requirements, to address safety 
and odour concerns, or for other reasons.  
Paragraph 5 states: 
This methodology is not applicable:  
(a) In combination with other approved 
methodologies. For instance, ACM0001 
cannot be used to claim emission reductions 
for the displacement of fossil fuels in a kiln 
or glass melting furnace, where the purpose 
of the CDM project activity is to implement 
energy efficiency measures at a kiln or glass 
melting furnace.  
(b) If the management of the SWDS in the 
project activity is deliberately changed during 
the crediting in order to increase methane 
generation compared to the situation prior to 
the implementation of the project activity.  
Paragraph 5: 
(a) Applicable. ACM0001 is applied with 
ACM0022 in the project. This applicability 
condition is proposed to prevent double 
counting with version 12. In the project 
activity, a portion of fresh waste (only MSW is 
separated and utilized on -site with same 
purpose of electricity gene ration. There are 
dedicated flowmeters to anaerobic reactors 
(for each reactor, 3 total) and to landfill site. 
Therefore, generated biogas is accounted 
separately. Leak emissions are also applied 
(especially for solid digestate) to prevent 
double counting since solid digestate is 
disposed to the landfill site.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
41 
(b) Applicable. The management of the 
SWDS in the project activity will not be 
deliberately changed during the crediting in 
order to increase methane generation 
compared to the situation prior to the 
implementation of the project activity.  In 
Türkiye each province is responsible for local 
waste management and allocates waste 
disposal to existing sites. In Izmir province, 
project activity is within the premises of a 
sanitary landfill, and the previous landfill lot  
(no longer accepting any waste)  is 
rehabilitated for better residual gas 
collection. Waste . According to 
environmental report of Izmir province26 
(report page 142), collected wastes are 
directed to four sanitary landfill of the Izmir 
province.

text_blocks.heading: 3.3 Project Boundary
text_blocks.text:
According to the ACM0022, the spatial extent of the project boundary is the SWDS where the 
waste is disposed of in the baseline, anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits treating organic wastewater 
in the baseline, and the site of the alternative waste treatment process(es). The boundary also 
includes on -site electricity generation and use, on -site fuel use and the wastewater treatment 
plant used to treat the w astewater by-products of the alternative waste treatment process(es). 
The project boundary does not include facilities for waste collection and transport.  
In the case that the project provides electricity to a grid, then the spatial extent of the project 
boundary will also include those plants connected to the energy system to which the plant is 
connected. If upgraded biogas is fed to a natural gas distribut ion system, then the natural gas 
distribution system is also included in the boundary.

(a) Sites where the LFG is flared or used (applicable),  
(b) Captive power plant(s) (including emergency diesel generators) or power generation sources 
connected to the grid, which are supplying electricity to the project activity (only diesel generator 
is applicable); 
(c) Captive power plant(s) (including emergency diesel generators) or power generation sources 
connected to the grid, which are supplying electricity in the baseline that is displaced by electricity 
generated by captured LFG in the project activity;  
(d) Heat generation equipment or sources which are supplying heat in the baseline that is 
displaced by heat generated by captured LFG in the project activity; and  
(e) The transportation of compressed/liquefied LFG from the biogas processing facility to 
consumers.
