Figure 6. Project Boundary 
 
Section 5.3 of applied methodology, titled “Procedure for estimating the end of the remaining 
lifetime of existing equipment” describes the procedure for used equipment.  
Paragraph 17 states: “ This procedure applies if LFG is used in equipment that was in operation 
prior to the implementation of the project activity. ”. Under project activity, LFG is only used in gas 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
63 
engines and flare. Both units are purchased exclusively for the project activity and not used in 
any facility before.  
Paragraph 18 states: “ For each item of equipment which was in operation prior to the 
implementation of the project activity and in which the captured LFG is used after the 
implementation of the project activity, project participants shall estimate its remaining lifetime 
by appl ying the “Tool to determine the remaining lifetime of equipment 30”. These items of 
equipment and their remaining lifetime shall be recorded in the CDM -PDD.”  Since all equipment 
that uses LFG has not been used in other projects before, this paragraph is not applicable.  
Paragraph 19 states: “ At the end of the remaining lifetime of each item of equipment, the 
procedure for the selection of the most plausible baseline scenario related to electricity and/or 
heat generation shall be updated in order to determine the most plausible baseline fuel that 
would be used after installation of the new equipment in the absence of the CDM project activity. 
At the same time, the parameters related to this item of equipmen t shall also be re -estimated 
according to the procedures in this met hodology used to make the original estimation (for 
example the baseline fuel may change, and this then has impacts on the emission factor for this 
baseline fuel).”.  
According to TOOL 10 of CDM, titled “Tool to determine the remaining lifetime of equipment” 
version 01 31 , default technical lifetime of gas fired generator sets are 50000h. This value is 
roughly equivalent to 7 years of operation. However, in practice with good maintenance, 
optimization and part replacement life time of equipment can be expanded. Project owner plans 
to use the gas engines for a duration of 15-20 years. Although, it is not expected for gas engine 
and flare to outlast the project activity (even without the renewal), project will evaluate the most 
plausible baseline scenario related to electricity and/or heat generation shall be updated in order 
to determine the most plausible baseline fuel that would be used after installation of the new 
equipment in the absence of the CDM project activity. At the sa me time, the parameters related 
to this item of equipment shall also be re -estimated according to the procedures in this 
methodology used to make the original estimation .

text_blocks.heading: 3.4 Baseline Scenario
text_blocks.text:
According to ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment processes --- Version 03.0, the following 
alternatives or combinations of these alternatives shall, inter alia, be considered for the baseline 
scenario of the fresh waste treatment:  
(a) M1: The project activity without being registered as a CDM project activity (i.e. any 
(combination) of the waste treatment processes listed in Table 2);

text_blocks.heading: 31 https://cdm.unfccc.int/methodologies/PAmethodologies/tools/am -tool-10-v1.pdf
P6 is a plausible scenario for project activity. Greenfield facility is connected to grid where there
text_blocks.text:
was no power generation from neither fossil fuel nor renewable sources.  
For the treatment of the wastewater, the following alternatives or combinations of these 
alternatives shall, inter alia, be considered:  
 
(a) W1: Continuation of current practice of using anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits without 
methane recovery;  
(b) W2: Anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits with methane recovery and flaring of the recovered 
methane;  
(c) W3: Anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits with methane recovery and utilization of the recovered 
methane for electricity and/or heat generation;  
(d) W4: Construction of a new anaerobic lagoon or sludge pits without methane recovery;  
(e) W5: Construction of a new anaerobic lagoon or sludge pits with methane recovery and flaring 
of the recovered methane;  
(f) W6: Using the wastewater for co -composting (the project activity implemented without being 
registered as a CDM project activity);  
(g) W7: Other treatment processes provided in table 6.3, Volume 5, chapter 6 of the IPCC 2006 
guidelines for greenhouse gas inventory.  
 
Treatment of the wastewater  is not included in the project activity, thus none of the options are 
applicable for baseline scenarios.  
For heat generation, one or a combination of the following alternatives, inter alia, shall be 
considered: 
(a) H1: Heat generated as a by -product from one of the processes for waste treatment listed in 
Table 1, not undertaken as a CDM project activity;  
(b) H2: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on -site or off -site fossil fuel fired 
cogeneration plant(s);5  
(c) H3: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on -site or off -site renewable based 
cogeneration plant(s);6  
(d) H4: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on -site or off-site fossil fuel-based boiler(s) 
or air heater(s); 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
66 
(e) H5: Use of (an) existing or construction of (a) new on -site or off-site renewable energy -based 
boiler(s) or air heater(s);  
(f) H6: District heat;  
(g) H7: Other heat generation technologies (e.g. heat pumps or solar energy).  
Treatment of the heat generation is not included in the project activity, thus none of the options 
are applicable for baseline scenarios.  
 
According to paragraph 20 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ Project participants shall 
demonstrate that the identified baseline fuel used for generation of heat is available in 
abundance in the host country and there is no supply constraint. In case of supply constraints 
(e.g. partial or seasonal supply), project p articipants shall consider the period when there are 
availability constraints and choose the fuel(s) that result in the most conservative estimation of 
baseline emissions.”. Since project activity does not include heat generation, this condition is not 
applicable.  
According to paragraph 21 of the applied methodology ACM0022,  “Detailed justifications shall 
be provided and documented in the CDM -PDD for the selected baseline fuel. As a conservative 
approach, the lowest carbon -intensive fuel, such as natural gas, may be used throughout all 
periods of the year.”. Since project activity does not include heat generation, this condition is not 
applicable.  
According to paragraph 22 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “For the supply of upgraded 
biogas to a natural gas distribution network, the baseline is assumed to be the continued supply 
with natural gas. ”. Since project activity does not include supply of upgraded biogas to a natural 
gas distribution network, this condition is not applicable.  
According to paragraph 23 of the applied methodology ACM0022,  “In applying Sub -step 1b of 
the tool, mandatory applicable legal and regulatory requirements may include mandatory LFG 
capture or destruction requirements because of safety issues or local environmental regulations. 
Other policies could include local polici es promoting productive use of LFG, such as those for the 
production of renewable energy, or those that promote the processing of fresh waste.”. In Türkiye 
there are no laws and regulations tha t require landfills to flare or utilize generated landfill gas. 
According to paragraph 24 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “If the products or by-products 
are used by another CDM project activity and if the project participants of both CDM projects are 
the same, then the following provision shall be followed:  
 
(a) The by-products shall be considered to have market value when assessing additionality of the 
project activity applying this methodology;  
(b) The by -products shall be considered to have no value when assessing additionality of the 
second project activity.” 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
67 
  
Project activity does not use products or by -products from another CDM activity. Therefore, this 
this condition is not applicable.  
 
According to paragraph 25 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ Project activities 
implementing greenfield composting facilities for the treatment of MSW are deemed 
automatically additional when  any of conditions below is fulfilled: (a) The host country is a LDC; 
or  
(b) MSW collection coverage is below 50 per cent for the applicable geographical region; or  
(c) MSW collection coverage is 50 –80 per cent for the applicable geographical region and the 
waste received by the project composting facility does not have formal segregation of wet and 
dry waste (i.e. excluding recycling by the informal sector); or  
(d) No gate/tipping fees are received by the project composting facility or any upstream waste 
collection or segregation facility which is contractually bound to provide waste to the project 
composting facility; or  
(e) Less than two per cent of the collected MSW of the municipality (or the region from where the 
MSW treated by the project activity is sourced) are treated by composting in the year before the 
PDD is published for global stakeholder consultation. ”  
Since project activity does not include composting or co -composting, this condition is not 
applicable. 
According to paragraph 26 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ For project activities that are 
deemed automatically additional above, the baseline scenario is assumed to be the continued 
disposal of the fresh waste in the nearest SWDS (where the LFG is released or captured and 
destructed through flaring to comply with  regulations or contractual requirements, to address 
safety and odour concerns, or for other reasons). If condition (d) or (e) applies, it shall also be 
demonstrated that 80 per cent of the collected MSW of the municipality (or the region from where 
the MSW treated by the project activity is sourced) are treated in SWDS or stockpiles or are 
dumped in the year before the PDD is published for global stakeholder consultation. ”  
Project activities that are associated with methodology ACM0022 are not automatically 
additional. Therefore, this condition is not applicable.  
According to paragraph 27 of the applied methodology ACM0022, “ The collection coverage of 
MSW may be estimated as: (1) the quantity of waste collected divided by the total waste 
generation, or (2) the population covered by waste collection service divided by the total 
population. The quantity of waste collected may be  (1) obtained from the municipal waste 
authority, (2) based on local statistics, or (3) based on the MSW accepted by all waste processing 
facilities, including open dump sites. Total waste gene ration may be calculated using data on 
population and/or waste generation per capita. ”. 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
68 
This condition is applicable.  Collection coverage of MSW is not used since project activity already 
set its capacity based on practice of Izmir municipality , which is responsible for waste 
management of whole Izmir province.  The Municipality  collects MSW from all districts of the 
province and treats fresh waste in proper existing facilities. The quantity  of collected waste is 
obtained from the local authorities. Weighbridge of Bergama solid waste management facility is 
under the authority of Izmir municipal ity thus direct data , local statistics and other relative data 
can be obtained from the relevant environmental agency.  
 
According to ACM00 01: Flaring or use of landfill gas --- Version 19.0 section 5.3, project 
participants may either apply the simplified procedure in section 5.3.1 or the procedures in 
section 5.3.2 to select the most plausible baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality.  
The simplified procedure is applied for the proposed project, and the baseline is assumed to be 
the atmospheric release of the LFG; all the electricity to be supplied by the project is assumed to 
be electricity generation in existing and/or new grid -connected power plants. 
According to paragraph 22 of the applied methodology ACM0001, “The baseline scenario for LFG 
is assumed to be the atmospheric release of the LFG or capture of LFG and destruction through 
flaring to comply with regulations or contractual requirements, to ad dress safety and odour 
concerns, or for other reasons”. The project baseline scenario for LFG is “the atmospheric release 
of the LFG”. 
Before the project activity, the landfill site was a sanitary landfill where biomass and other organic 
matter content of MSW is  left to decay within the project boundary, and generated methane 
emitted to the atmosphere in the absence of the project activity. There were no flaring nor energy 
generation at the landfill site. 
According to paragraph 23 of the applied methodology ACM0001, “If all or part of the electricity 
generated by the project activity is exported to the grid, the baseline scenario for all or the part 
of the electricity exported to the grid is assumed to be e lectricity generation in existing and/or 
new grid-connected power plants. If all or part of the electricity is supplied to off -grid application, 
the baseline electricity generation equipment is assumed to correspond to the default emission 
factor from Opti on B2 of the "Methodological tool: Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions 
from electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity generation”.  
According to this explanation, the most plausible baseline scenario for electricity generation is

text_blocks.heading: P6 “electricity generation in existing and/or new grid -connected power plants”.
3.5 Additionality
text_blocks.text:
Additionality of project activity is considered for each methodology. Project activities that are 
associated with ACM0001: Flaring or use of landfill gas --- Version 19.0  are stays within the 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
69 
TOOL32 Positive List and additionality is investigated for project activities that are associated 
with ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment processes - Version 03.0  
Additionality analysis for ACM0001: Flaring or use of landfill gas --- Version 19.0 
According to the section 5.3.1 of the methodology ACM0001, the project chooses simplified 
procedures to demonstrate additionality. Tool 32 (Positive lists of technologies) is used to 
demonstrate additionality.  
Section 5 of this tool contains a positive list of technologies, which are deemed automatically 
additional if project activities exclusively apply the technologies listed and demonstrate that they 
fulfil the related conditions. Paragraph 11 of the tool specifies landfill gas recovery and its gainful 
use: 
“The project activities and PoAs at new or existing landfills (greenfield or brownfield) are deemed 
automatically additional, if it is demonstrated  that prior to the implementation of the project 
activities and PoAs the landfill gas (LFG) was only vented and/or flared (in the case of brownfield 
projects) or would have been only vented and/or flared (in the case of greenfield projects) but 
not utilize d for energy generation, and that under the project activities and PoAs any of the 
following conditions are m et: 
• The LFG is used to generate electricity in one or several power plants with a total nameplate 
capacity that equals or is below 10 MW;  
• The LFG is used to generate heat for internal or external consumption;  
• The LFG is flared.  
Prior to the implementation of the project,  the generated LFG was vented to atmosphere without 
implementation of methane destruction 323334. With the project implementation, biogas is 
collected and utilized in electricity production. Since the installed licence capacity 9.898 MWe is 
below 10 MWe, the project is automatically additional.  
It is concluded that the project meets the requirements of TOOL 32 and is additional.  
Additionality analysis for ACM0022: Alternative waste treatment processes - Version 03.0 
In the case of the proposed project activity, the Methodology of ACM0022: Alternative waste 
treatment processes, version 03.035 is applied to the proposed project activity. Additionality for 
the project activity is demonstrated using Tool 02 Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario

STEP 2 - Barrier analysis;  
There are no technology barriers, acceptability barriers nor financial barriers that may prevent 
these two alternative scenarios from occurring. As per Tool 02 Combined tool to identify the 
baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality (Version 07.0), go to Step 3 investment analysis. 
STEP 3 – Investment analysis;  
The investment analysis has been done in order to make an economic and financial evaluation 
of the project. No public funding or ODA are available in Turkey for finance of this type of projects. 
For investment analysis, loan conditions have been determined  considering the average market 
rates/term sheets signed with the banks. Investment analysis is carried as per the 
methodological Tool 2: “ Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate 
additionality”, Version 07.0.  
Sub-step 2a - Determine appropriate analysis method  
There are three options for the determination of analysis method which are:  
• Simple Cost Analysis  
• Investment Comparison Analysis and  
• Benchmark Analysis 
Since Project generates economic benefits from sales of electricity, the simple cost analysis is 
not applicable. Also, since the baseline of the project is generation of electricity by the grid, no 
alternative investment is considered at issue. So, it has been decided to use benchmark analysis 
for evaluation of the project investment.  
Sub-step 2b: Option III. Apply benchmark analysis  
For benchmark analysis, figure defined by World Bank for similar project types have been used 
which has been given as 20% (pre -tax)37 for IRR by a report generated in June 2017. For the 
proposed project, in order to reach this IRR values, average electricity tariff must be above current

text_blocks.heading: 37 http://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/799701498842988254/pdf/ICR00004069 -06192017.pdf
text_blocks.text:
Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
73 
YEKDEM tariffs in the absence of carbon revenue and assuming that initial investment figures 
are realized so that the investment will become reasonable.  
 
Sub-step 2c. Calculation and comparison of financial indicators  
Table 1 Financial Parameters used in Investment Analysis  
Installed Power MWe 7.07 
Tariff Rate USD/ MWh

Annual electricity 
generation MWh 53,058.172

The technical lifetime of Bergama integrated solid waste management facility and electricity 
generation facility  is determined from the operational agreement, the economic lifetime is 
determined as 20 years. The fair value has been included in the project IRR calculation. The 
depreciation years have been determined as per the local regulation 38. Finally, in the IRR 
calculation the date specified in the tool was used .  
The World Bank has established a standard for renewable energy investments in Turkey. It has 
an IRR threshold benchmark, which is the minimum IRR necessary for project finance. As a result, 
it is a "commercial lending rate," as defined by T OOL27. The benchmark IRR was derived from a 
World Bank loan to the Turkish Renewable Energy Sector under the Clean Technology Fund (CTF). 
The suggested CTF benchmark was found to be compatible with the qualifying requirements for 
emission reduction projects ( that is, sign ificant potential in emission reductions, demonstration 
potential, development impact and implementation potential). For each project type, threshold 
IRRs have been computed, which are the lowest IRRs required to attract investors.  
Based on an analysis of prototype sub -projects (see Table 3.3 page 40), it was determined that 
CTF resources would be made available for newer RE technologies and EE investments, and for

text_blocks.heading: 38 https://www.gib.gov.tr/sites/default/files/fileadmin/user_upload/Yararli_Bilgiler/amortisman_oranlari.pdf
text_blocks.text:
Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
74 
smaller-scale hydro and wind projects, financial barriers existed which reduced the rates of return 
on equity (IRRs) below the levels required to attract investors. The desired thresholds were 
assessed as follows: 15% for small hydro, 20% biomass, and 25 %  for solar.39 
Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the Bergama integrated solid waste management facility and 
electricity generation facility  has been calculated as 12.753% based on the parameters given 
above without considering the carbon revenue. The input values of all financial analysis are 
derived from the company feasibility report sheet which was completed by the project owner. 
Investment decision date was accepted a s credit loan  agreement date of 11-May-2020. As 
demonstrated feasibility sheet, operation starts in 202 0 with 2 units and another 4 units 
commissioned up to 202 3. It’s expected that the final gas engine will be commissioned in year

text_blocks.heading: 2024. Waste management of Izmir province is decided by Izmir municipality. Plant load factor will
text_blocks.text:
increase by years with more MSW is directed to the facility by the Izmir Municipality. In the facility, 
several systems are involved where different revenues and expenses occur. In the IRR excel, 
under the “O&M” and “ Electricity-Carbon& RDF Revenue ”, sheets, breakdown of costs and 
revenues can be found within operation years as RDF and Waste disposal facility revenues 
included in the equity IRR. The project does not use any ODA or gov ernment incentive. Electricity 
tariff has been used as $133 USD /MWh for 10 years according to YEKDEM mechanism starting 
from 31-December-2020 and after this period electricity price assumed to be spot price of 
market. Market spot price has been founded as the average of last 6 years ( 2018 to 08 -July-
2024), which is 71.535 USD/MWh. This value is depending on PTF price in Turkey, and it is very 
volatile and unforeseeable which is determining related to supply – demand price on that specific 
day. This price is almost impossible to make long term forecast because of unstable and 
unreliable economic environment in Turkey. This Tariff assumption is done technically. 
Additionally, the project owner applied for the YEKDEM mechanism in October 202 0 to benefit 
from the 202 1 YEKDEM feed -in tariff. As per Regulation, request for registration to YEKDEM 
should be realized every December. Please see The IRR excel sheet for the demonstration of the 
calculation. 
The annual generation has been taken as 53,058.17 MWh as calculated in the feasibility sheet 
in line with generation license and commissioned units. This generation value by years are  
demonstrated in detail in IRR excel “ Electricity-Carbon& RDF Revenue” sheet. In this excel sheet 
details of first year production are calculated.  
The project IRR of Bergama integrated solid waste management facility and electricity generation 
facility has been calculated on the basis of expected cash flows (investment, operating costs and 
revenues from electricity sale, waste disposal and RDF facility), as determined in the financial 
feasibility assessment of the project. The parameters and values used for the I RR calculation will 
be provided to VCS DOE, if needed. The resulting IRR for 20 years is 12.753%

text_blocks.heading: 39 http://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/799701498842988254/pdf/ICR00004069 -06192017.pdf
text_blocks.text:
Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
75 
Acceptable IRR values for energy investments in Turkey are, given the present global financial 
crisis and economic uncertainty, are expected at yields in excess of 20% per annum. Even if we 
include the carbon revenue in the cash flow (which is, IRR increas es to 13.540% from 12.753%) 
which is still lower than the accepted benchmark rates. Considering the market price risk, it can 
be concluded the extension will make the investment more feasible, however, it will still be below 
the benchmark IRR. 
Sub-step 2d – Sensitivity Analysis  
Sensitivity analysis was carrie d out for three main parameters identified for the first phase of the 
project. Since the investment cost of the project is not changed, only impact of change in tariff 
and operating cost has been included in sensitivity analysis.  
While the main parameter determining the income of the project is the electricity sales revenue, 
investment cost and operation cost, a variation of the accordant values shall demonstrate the 
reliability of the IRR calculation. Applying variations on invest ment costs, the range was 
considered to be in a bandwidth of +10% and –10% as advised in “Tool27 - Investment Analysis 
(ver 07.0).” 
The sensitivity analysis confirms that the proposed project activity is unlikely to be economically 
attractive without the revenues from VER as even the maximum IRR result ( 13.540%) for the 
best-case scenario is below the benchmark, which is 10%. In this scenario, increase of revenue 
up to %10 is limited for electricity revenue by the local regulation discussed below in details. 
Electricity revenues have majority in the total revenues however other revenues are also effective 
where they depend on the waste amount which are fixed by agreements. Also, for electricity 
revenue, according to YEKDEM pricing, the fixed tar iff is determined for a 10 years period. Since 
there is a fixed price and following to it ceiling price for renewable energy power plants, and waste 
amount which were already determined by agreements there is no chance that the revenue will 
be +10%. 
The project has already financed with project owner equity and due to the fact that the project 
owner is the distributor of the electronic components in Turkey so, own resources are used in this 
project that means there is no chance that the investment cos t will be -%10. In addition to that, 
Turkey has feed in tariff which means fixed electricity price for renewable project, it is unlikely to 
have a higher electricity price, which is above the market price.

Outcome of Step 2:  
The investment and sensitivity analysis shows that the VER revenues will improve the financial 
indicators of the Project remarkably. Considering that figures above are based on a higher price 
rather than the government guaranteed floor price, optimistic es timations for yearly generation 
and that those figures do not reflect the risk for investment, role of carbon income is a most 
significant number to enable the project to proceed.  
According to local regulations, electricity price is determined daily according to Market Financial 
Settlement Centre (MFSC) as defined in the regulations and there exists three tariffs during day, 
peak and night hours. The value does not provide any guara ntee about the actual selling price 
as the control on generation period and tariff is limited and it may not be possible to generate 
and sell electricity during peak tariff periods. Also, considering that fluctuation and dependence 
in sunshine is high and fact that a part of the electricity can be sold through bilateral agreements 
to free consumers with a discount rate over market price, guarantee price has been taken as 
reference in investment analysis which also provides input for evaluation of financing institutions. 
However, the latest regulation will have inhibitor effect on renewable energy -based project as 
described below. 
The latest application from the Turkish government announced on the official gazette Resource -
Based Ceiling Price Determination for EMRA Licensed Production Facilities” 40 is setting a ceiling 
price limit due to the increasing cost of energy -related raw materials and uncertainty of Russia -
Ukraine situation related to daily market clearance price (PTF) 41 and YEKDEM price.

text_blocks.heading: 40 https://www.gensed.org/assets/attachments/dosyalar/EPDK -Tavan-Fiyat-Uygulamas%C4%B1na -%C4%B0li%C5%9Fkin -
text_blocks.text: Ayr%C4%B1nt%C4%B1lar%C4%B1 -Duyurdu.pdf

text_blocks.heading: 41 https://seffaflik.epias.com.tr/transparency/piyasalar/gop/ptf.xhtml
text_blocks.text:
Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
77 
Currently, as a YEKDEM system, Biomass and Biogas Power Plants have an agreement on a 13.3 
$c /kWh (equal to 133 USD/MWh) price. There is a new regulation that sets the ceiling price as 
1,200 TL/ MWh for renewables and 2,500 TL/ MWh for fossil -fueled power plants. At the time of 
announcement those values are approximately 80 USD/MWh for renewables and 120 USD/MWh 
for fossil-fueled plants. The price difference between the barrier and PTF price will be charged as 
tax from renewable energy source owners to sup port fossil fuels plant owners. Which is a kind of 
punishment system for renewables especially at this hard time of investment and reward system 
for coal and natural gas -based power plants. Although this practice is designed to protect and 
ensure the deman d for energy and secure consumers, it will directly harm the producers, 
especially renewable energy -based producers 
Another important parameter affecting IRR is investment cost. However, since the agreements 
have been made and costs are realized as given in the financial model, there is no chance of 
expecting a decrease in the investment cost due to the inflation rate w hich also considered in 
the feasibility report. Operating costs can also affect the IRR. However, its impact is not significant 
and does not cause any significant change in IRR, and the fluctuation percentage to reach the 
benchmark is very high and not lik ely. Based on the above information, it is seen that project is 
not the most attractive option. Therefore, the project is considered an addition to the baseline 
scenario. 
 
Step 3: Common Practice Analysis  
The section below provides the Common Practice Analysis as step 4 of the “Combined tool to 
identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate additionality, version 7.0” and “Common Practice, 
version 03.1”.  
Step 1: Calculate applicable capacity or output range as +/ - 50% of the total design capacity or 
output of the proposed project activity  
The project’s installed capacity is 7.07 MWe. Therefore, the applicable capacity range for the 
analysis will be 3.535 MWe – 10.605 MWe. 
Step 2: Identify similar projects (both CDM and non -CDM) which fulfill all of the following 
conditions:  
(a) The projects are located in the applicable geographical area;  
(b) The projects apply the same measure as the proposed project activity;  
(c) The projects use the same energy source/fuel and feedstock as the proposed project activity, 
if a technology switch measure is implemented by the proposed project activity;  
(d) The plants in which the projects are implemented produce goods or services with comparable 
quality, properties, and applications areas (e.g. clinker) as the proposed project plant;  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
78 
(e) The capacity or output of the projects is within the applicable capacity or output range 
calculated in Step 1;  
(f) The projects started commercial operation before the project design document (CDM -PDD) is 
published for global stakeholder consultation or before the start date of proposed project activity, 
whichever is earlier for the proposed project activity.  
Step 3: Within the projects identified in Step 2, identify those that are neither registered CDM 
project activities, project activities submitted for registration, nor project activities undergoing 
validation. Note their number N all. 
 
Step-2 (a to f) and Step 3:  
 
The start date of the Project Activity is 31 -October-2020. Therefore, projects which have started 
commercial operation before 31-October-2020, have been considered for analysis. The most up -
to-date list of the plants operating in Turkey have been developed by adding new projects until 
end of 202 3 on the list of existing projects given by yearly report of energy investment list 
published by The Ministry of Energy and Natural Resources in Turkey from year 2012. Since the 
project activity produces electricity,  electricity generation companies are considered for the 
analysis. The plants whose capacities are between 3.535 MWe and 10.605 MWe were included. 
The projects which have the same measure and company type are included in the list ( biomass 
based renewable energy projects, no distinction between fuels ), and the projects registered as 
VER projects and projects under validation are excluded. The number of remaining power plants, 
Nall, is 23. As stated in Step 2- c) chosen list only consists of energy projects since in the step 2 
calculation only renewable energy sources in Turkey taken since there is no technology switch 
measure implementation, this step is not applicable.  Both Nall and Similar projects list are 
displayed in Appendix 3 and 4.  
Step 4: within similar projects identified in Step 3, identify those that apply technologies that are 
different to the technology applied in the proposed project activity. Note their number N diff.  
All projects that use MSW are similar projects . There are three similar projects.  
Thus, Ndiff = 23-20=3. 
Step 5: calculate factor F = 1 - Ndiff / Nall representing the share of similar projects (penetration 
rate of the measure/technology) using a measure/technology similar to the measure/technology 
used in the proposed project activity that deliver the same output or capacity as the proposed 
project activity. 
F = 1 - (Ndiff / Nall)  
F = 1 – (20/23) = 0.130 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
79 
Nall - Ndiff = 23 - 20 = 3 
The proposed project activity is a “common practice” within a sector in the applicable 
geographical area if the factor F is greater than 0.2 and N all-Ndiff is greater than 3.  
Since Nall - Ndiff = 3 and F=0.130, the project activity is not a common practice and so, the project 
is additional.

There are no methodological deviations. 
4 IMPLEMENTATION STATUS

text_blocks.heading: 4.1 Implementation Status of the Project Activity
text_blocks.text:
The project site is located within the boundaries of the II. Class Bergama Solid Waste Landfill 
Facility and Landfill gas (LFG) to power facility integrated into the existing II. Class Solid Waste 
Landfill Facility.  
Waste acceptance to the sanitary landfill started in 2011. The facility is originally planned to 
include two additional lots. Originally landfill was planned to accept MSW from only Bergama and 
Dikili districts however in time MSW is accepted from more districts. Izmir province has an aging 
major landfill called Harmandalı and its slowly fulfilling its lifespan. Therefore, more MSW is 
directed to Bergama than original plan, including Kınık District and Aliağa District. The daily waste 
quantum of 435 tons/day (2020) is planned to reach 1200 tons/day in 2027. This situation is 
reflected in the recent EIA revision where a third lot is licensed.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
80 
 
Figure 7. Flow Diagram of Facility  
In 2014, The Bergama Solid Waste Disposal Facility was handed over to the Izmir Metropolitan 
Municipality. In 2019 Izmir Metropolitan Municipality handed over the operation and 
responsibility of modern solid waste management systems installation to the  İzmir Doğu Star 
Elektrik Üretim A.Ş  which is sub -company of Biotrend. After the handover, the facility was 
transformed into an Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility. Mechanical Separation Facility, 
Biomethanization Facility, Compost Facility and Electricity Generation Facilities were a dded. 
In line with the sustainable integrated waste management planned within the scope of the 
project, the packaging wastes are collected separately at the source and sent to the collection 
and sorting facilities licensed by the Ministry of Environment and Urba nization, and they will be 
sorted according to their types there. Packaging waste to be collected separately at their source 
will not be accepted to the facility or any sanitary landfill.  
The project, Bergama Integrated Solid Waste Management Facility and Electricity Generation 
Facility (10.157 MWm / 9.898 MWe / 13.895 MWt) is in Izmir Province, Bergama District, Kaşıkçı 
Neighborhood, and owned and operated by İzmir Doğu Star Elektrik Üretim A.Ş.  
The project site is located within the boundaries of the II. Class Bergama Solid Waste Landfill 
Facility. It is planned to integrate the facilities to be established within the scope of the project 
and the existing II. Class Solid Waste Landfill Facility.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
81 
After the mixed municipal wastes coming to the facility are separated according to their types at 
the Mechanical Separation Facility, the biodegradable wastes will be taken to the 
Biomethanization Facility, the remaining wastes will be sent to the existing  sanitary landfill and 
the recyclable wastes will be sent to licensed companies. It’s estimated that about 20% of the 
biodegradable material cannot be separated and sent to landfill. The biogas to be obtained from 
the Biomethanization Facility and the meth ane gas to be extracted from the sanitary landfill will 
be converted into electrical energy at the Electricity Generation Facility and transferred to the 
interconnected system to generate income.  
In line with the sustainable integrated waste management planned within the scope of the 
project, the packaging wastes to be collected separately at the source will be sent to the 
collection and sorting facilities licensed by the Ministry of Environment an d Urbanization, and 
they will be sorted according to their types there. Packaging waste to be collected separately at 
their source will not be accepted to the facility or any sanitary landfill.  
RDF production  and capacity increase for mechanical separation units are expected to be in 
service as of January 2024 therefore RDF process is also accounted for after the 
beforementioned date. 
It's estimated that at full capacity, annual net electricity generation will reach 74,235 MWh and 
corresponding annual estimated emission reduction is 196,867 tCO2e as average. The starting 
date of the project activity shall be considered as 31 -October-2020 which is the provisional 
acceptance date of the first two gas engines.  
The Ministry of Environment and Urbanization has authorized the Environmental Impact 
Assessment (EIA) Report. According to Turkish rules and regulations, an EIA's permission may 
only be granted if the project in question has no detrimental effects on the e nvironment or the 
economy, thus the project has no net harm. All kinds of preventive measures will be taken to 
minimize the odor, dust, leachate, gas and similar negative effects that may arise from the facility. 
These preventive measures are explained in the Environmental  Impact Assessment Report in 
further detail.  
There were delays with data recording during first crediting period and flowmeter assigned to 
power generation from only LFG stopped gas counting on 13 -April-2023. Data substitution used 
is used to fill gaps for intermittent data recording, however no base line emissions from 
methane at SWDS is counted for LFG after 13 -April-2023. 
Biogas generated by digesters are counted at two locations, first at the outlet of anaerobic 
digester and then with the booster unit. Amount of methane produced by digester is calculated 
with the flow meters placed at the outlet of digesters and, used for calculations of project and 
leakage emissions.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
82 
5 ESTIMATED GHG EMISSION 
REDUCTIONS AND REMOVALS

Baseline emissions of methane from the SWDS (BE CH4,t,y): 
As per Tool 04, the amount of methane generated from disposal of waste at the SWDS is 
calculated based on a first order decay (FOD) model. The model differentiates between the 
different types of waste j with respective constant decay rates (k j) and fractions of degradable

organic carbon (DOC j). Application B is used for baseline emissions for biomethanization facility, 
according to the ACM0022.  
Baseline methane emissions from the SWDS are determined using “TOOL04: Emissions from 
solid waste disposal sites”. The following requirements shall be complied with when applying the 
tool: 
a) W j,x in the tool is the amount of organic fresh waste prevented from disposal in the baseline 
SWDS due to its treatment in any (combination) alternative waste treatment process;  
(b) Emission amounts are calculated using Application B in the tool (only fresh waste avoided 
from disposal after the start of the first crediting period shall be considered;  
(c) Sampling to determine the fractions of different waste types is necessary (note that for the 
case that the waste is combusted in the project activity, then the parameter Q j,c,y in this 
methodology is equivalent to the variable W j,x in the tool); 
(d) The tool instructs that f y shall be determined based on historic data or contract or regulation 
requirements specifying the amount of methane that must be destroyed/used (if available). The 
following additional instruction applies:  
(i) If the regulation requirements specify a percentage of the LFG that is required to be flared, the 
amount shall equal f y.; 
(ii) If the regulation requirements do not specify the amount or percentage of LFG that should be 
destroyed but require the installation of a capture system, without requiring the captured LFG to 
be flared then f y = 0; and 
(iii) If the requirement does not specify any amount or percentage of LFG that should be destroyed 
but require the installation of a system to capture and flare the LFG, then it is assumed f y = 0.2. 
The amount of methane generated from disposal of waste at the SWDS is calculated for year y 
with the following equation:

Baseline emissions from separate generation of electricity (BEEC,y) 
 
The baseline emissions associated with electricity generation in year y (BEEC,y) shall be calculated 
using  “TOOL05: Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions from electricity consumption and 
monitoring of electricity generation” . BEEN,t,y is equal to BEEC,y and baseline emissions from

When applying the tool:  
The electricity sources  k in the tool correspond to the sources of electricity generated identified

treatment process t and exported to the grid or displacing fossil fuel fired captive energy plant in 
year y (EGt,y). 
The project consumes electricity from the grid therefore the tool is applicable. (Scenario A).

(a) As per the gaseous stream tool, if the LFG is used for multiple purposes (e.g. flaring or energy

destruction devices shall be used as the destruction efficiency for all destruction devices 
monitored by this flow meter. If there are any periods for which one or more d estruction devices 
are not operational, paragraph 5 (a) and (b) of the Appendix of the "Tool to determine the mass 
flow of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream" tool shall be followed;  
(b) CH4 is the greenhouse gas for which the mass flow should be determined;  
(c) The simplification offered for calculating the molecular mass of the gaseous stream is valid 
(equations (3) or (17) in the tool);  
(d) The mass flow should be calculated on an hourly basis for each hour h in year y;  
(e) The mass flow calculated for hour h is 0 if the equipment is not working in hour h (O pj,h=not 
working), the hourly values are then summed to a yearly unit basis.

vi,t,db  = Volumetric fraction of greenhouse gas i in the gaseous stream in a time interval t on a 
dry basis (m³ gas i/m³ dry gas)

Ex ante estimation of F CH4,PJ,y  
Paragraph 37 of applied methodology ACM0001 states that, An ex ante estimate of F CH4,PJ,y is 
required to estimate baseline emission of methane from the SWDS (according to equation (2)) in 
order to estimate the emission reductions of the proposed project activity in the CDM -PDD. It is 
determined as follows 
 
FCH4,PJ,y = (ηPJ × BECH4,SWDS,y)/ GWPCH4 Equation (5) 
Where:

activity in year y (tCH 4/yr) 
BECH4,SWDS,y = Amount of methane in the LFG that is generated from the SWDS in the baseline 
scenario in year y (tCO 2e/yr)  
ηPJ  = Efficiency of the LFG capture system that will be installed in the project activity  
GWPCH4  = Global warming potential of CH 4 (tCO2e/tCH4) 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
92 
The amount of methane generated from disposal of waste at the SWDS is calculated for year y 
(BECH4,SWDS) 
Paragraph 38 of applied methodology ACM0001 states that, BECH4,SWDS,y is determined using the 
methodological tool “Emissions from solid waste disposal sites”. The following guidance should 
be taken into account when applying the tool:  
 
a. fy in the tool shall be assigned a value of 0 because the amount of LFG that would 
have been captured and destroyed is already accounted for in equation (2) of this 
methodology;  
 
b. In the tool, x begins with the year that the SWDS started receiving wastes (e.g. the 
first year of SWDS operation); and  
c. Sampling to determine the fractions of different waste types is not necessary 
because the waste composition can be obtained from previous studies.

text_blocks.heading: 5.2 Project Emissions
text_blocks.text:
The project emissions in year  y are calculated for each alternative waste treatment process 
(ACM0022 methodology) implemented in the project activity as follows:  
𝑃𝐸𝑦 = 𝑃𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀𝑃,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐺𝐴𝑆,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐼𝑁𝐶,𝑦 (Equation 17) 
Where: 
𝑃𝐸𝑦 = Project emissions in year  y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀𝑃,𝑦 = Project emissions from composting or co -composting in year  y 
(t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = Project emissions from anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion 
in year y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐺𝐴𝑆,𝑦 = Project emissions from gasification in year  y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Project emissions associated with RDF/SB in year  y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐼𝑁𝐶,𝑦 = Project emissions from incineration in year  y (t CO2e) 
 
Since the project activity only includes anaerobic digestion, RDF production and biogas utilization 
for electricity, PECOMP,y, PEGAS,y and PEINC,y are zero.  
Project emissions from anaerobic digestion (PEAD,y) 
PEAD,y is calculated according to  “TOOL14: Project and leakage emissions from anaerobic 
digesters”.  
𝑃𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = 𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐶,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐿𝐴𝑅𝐸,𝑦 (Equation 1) 
 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
95 
Where: 
𝑃𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = Project emissions associated with the anaerobic digester in year y  
(t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 = Project emissions from electricity consumption associated with 
the anaerobic digester in year y (t CO 2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐶,𝑦 = Project emissions from fossil fuel consumption associated with 
the anaerobic digester in year y (t CO 2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 = Project emissions of methane from the anaerobic digester in year 
y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐿𝐴𝑅𝐸,𝑦 = Project emissions from flaring of biogas in year y (t CO 2e) 
 
PEFC,y is zero since there is no dedicated unit or process for anaerobic digestion processes  and 
diesel fuel consumption of generators are counted cumulatively with ACM0001 methodology 
power generation. There are three diesel generators operating on -site. 
PEFLARE,y is zero since flare unit is coupled with booster and counted cumulatively with ACM0001 
methodology power generation.  
PEEC,y is also considered zero since electricity consumption is counted cumulatively within the 
facility. 
PECH4,y is estimated with default values for ex -ante calculations and is Q biogas,y is monitored for 
calculating project emissions. Therefore option 1 is used for project emission calculations of 
section 7. 
𝑄𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 = 𝑄𝑏𝑖𝑜𝑔𝑎𝑠,𝑦 𝑥 𝑓𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 𝑥 𝜌𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 (Equation 2) 
Where:  
QCH4,y = Quantity of methane produced in the digester in year y (t CH 4) 
Qbiogas,y = Amount of biogas collected at the digester outlet in year y (Nm 3 biogas) 
fCH4,default = Default value for the fraction of methane in the biogas (m 3 CH4 / m3 biogas)  
ρCH4,y = Density of methane at normal conditions (t CH 4 / Nm3 CH4) 
 
ESTIMATED ANNUAL BIOGAS GENERATION) (from 
anaerobic digesters43) 
Year Value (tCH4/year)

Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
96

Flowmeters located at the outlet of digesters , record the QCH4 value (Amount of biogas collected 
at the digester outlet in year y, cumulatively).  
𝑃𝐸𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 = 𝑄𝐶𝐻4,𝑦 𝑥 𝐸𝐹𝐶𝐻4,𝑑𝑒𝑓𝑎𝑢𝑙𝑡 𝑥 𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 (Equation 4) 
Where; 
PECH4,y = Project emissions of methane from the anaerobic digester in year y (t CO 2e) 
QCH4,y = Quantity of methane produced in the anaerobic digester in year y (t CH 4)  
EFCH4,default = Default emission factor for the fraction of CH 4 produced that leaks from the 
anaerobic digester (fraction)  
GWPCH4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (t CO2 / t CH4) 
Project emissions associated with RDF/SB in year  y (PERDF_SB,y) 
𝑃𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = 𝑃𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀, 𝑅𝐷𝐹𝑆𝐵, 𝑦 +  𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶, 𝑅𝐷𝐹𝑆𝐵, 𝑦 𝑥 𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐶, 𝑅𝐷𝐹𝑆𝐵, 𝑦
+  𝑃𝐸𝑤𝑤, 𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵, 𝑦  
(Equation 19) 
 
Where: 
𝑃𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Project emissions associated with RDF/SB in year y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀,𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Project emissions from combustion of fossil waste associated with 
combustion of RDF/SB within the project boundary in year y (t CO2)  
𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Project emissions from electricity consumption associated with 
RDF/SB (production and on-site combustion) in year y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐶,𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Project emissions from fossil fuel consumption associated with 
RDF/SB (production and on-site combustion) in year y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝑤𝑤,𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Project emissions from the wastewater treatment associated with 
RDF/SB (production and on-site combustion) in year y (t CH4)  
 
PECOM,RDF_SB,y is considered zero since there is no dedicated unit or process for refuse drive fuel 
production and diesel fuel consumption of generators are counted cumulatively with ACM0001 
methodology power generation.  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
97 
PEEC,RDF_SB,y is considered zero since electricity consumption is counted cumulatively within the 
facility. 
PEWW,RDF_SB,y is considered zero since there is no wastewater generated via refuse drive fuel 
production. 
PEFC,RDF,y is zero since there is no dedicated unit or process for refuse drive fuel production and 
diesel fuel consumption of generators are counted cumulatively with ACM0001 methodology 
power generation. 
Therefore PERDF_SB,y is considered zero. 
The project emissions in year  y are calculated for the ACM0001 methodology implementation of 
the project activity as follows:  
𝑃𝐸𝑦 = 𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐶,𝑦 + 𝑃𝐸𝐷𝑇,𝑦+ 𝑃𝐸𝑆𝑃,𝑦+ 𝑃𝐸𝑓𝑙𝑎𝑟𝑒,𝑦 Equation (22) 
Where: 
𝑃𝐸𝑦 = Project emissions in year y (t CO2/yr) 
𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 = Emissions from consumption of electricity due to the project activity in 
year y (t CO2/yr) 
𝑃𝐸𝐹𝐶,𝑦 = Emissions from consumption of fossil fuels due to the project activity, for 
purpose other than electricity generation, in year y (t CO2/yr) 
𝑃𝐸𝐷𝑇,𝑦 = Emissions from the distribution of compressed/liquefied LFG using trucks, 
in year y (t CO2/yr) 
𝑃𝐸𝑆𝑃,𝑦 =   Emissions from the supply of LFG to consumers through a dedicated      
  pipeline, in year y (t CO2/yr) 
𝑃𝐸𝑓𝑙𝑎𝑟𝑒,𝑦                  =   Project emissions from flaring of the residual gas in year y (t CO2/yr) 
 
PESP,y and PEDT,y  are considered zero since generated LFG is used in site for power generation.  
For the methodology ACM0001 methodology, project emissions are calculated as below:  
 
PEy =  PEEC,y + PEFC,y +  PEDT,y + PESP,y  
 
Where: 
PEy = Project emissions in year y (tCO 2/yr) 
PEEC,y = Emissions from consumption of electricity due to the project activity in year y (tCO 2/yr) 
PEFC,y = Are the CO2 emissions from fossil fuel combustion in process j during the year y (tCO2/yr) 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
98 
PEDT,y = Emissions from the distribution of compressed/liquefied LFG using trucks, in year y 
(tCO2/yr) 
PESP,y = Emissions from the supply of LFG to consumers through a dedicated pipeline, in year y 
(tCO2/yr) 
The project does not involve the distribution of compressed/liquefied LFG using trucks nor 
pipelines. Therefore, PEDT,y, PESP,y is zero. Project includes an open flare and project emissions 
from flaring will be accounted for  (PEFlare,y). 
Hence, PEy is equal to summation of project emissions  PEEC,y + PEFC,y  + PEflare,y. 
PEy =  PEEC,y + PEFC,y +  PEflare,y  
 
Project emissions from consumption of electricity due to the project activity  (PEEC,y)  
The project emissions from electricity consumption due to waste treatment process t 
implemented under the project activity ( PEEC,y) shall be calculated using  “TOOL05: Baseline, 
project and/or leakage emissions from electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity 
generation”. This value also includes PE EC,RDF_SB,y and PE EC,y (electricity consumption from 
anerobic digester equipment).  
𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 = ∑ 𝐸𝐶𝑃𝐽,𝑗,𝑦 × 𝐸𝐹𝐸𝐹,𝑗,𝑦 × (1 + 𝑇𝐷𝐿𝑗,𝑦)
𝑗
 (Equation 1) 
 
Where: 
𝑃𝐸𝐸𝐶,𝑦 = Project emissions from electricity consumption in year y (t CO 2 / yr) 
𝐸𝐶𝑃𝐽,𝑗,𝑦 = Quantity of electricity consumed by the project electricity consumption source j in year 
y (MWh/yr) 
𝐸𝐹𝐸𝐹,𝑗,𝑦 = Emission factor for electricity generation for source j in year y (t CO 2/MWh) 
𝑇𝐷𝐿𝑗,𝑦 = Average technical transmission and distribution losses for providing electricity to source 
j in year y 
j = Sources of electricity consumption in the project  
 
As previously mentioned, emission factor for electricity generation for the proposed project 
activity is taken as the national EF published by Turkish Republic Ministry of energy.  Average 
technical transmission and distribution losses  also belongs to national value of Turkish national 
electricity grid as per Scenario A, option 1.  of TOOL05. 
 
 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
99 
Project emissions from fossil fuel combustion  (PEFC,j,y) 
According to regulation there is to have a captive power plant or diesel generator in case of 
shortage on methane for electricity production in case the electricity needs to be maintained and 
the engines don’t provide it temporarily.  
According to TOOL03 v03.0, CO2 emissions from fossil fuel combustion in process j are calculated 
based on the quantity of fuels combusted and the CO 2 emission coefficient of those fuels, as 
follows: 
PEFC,j,y = ∑iFCi,j,y x COEFi,y Equation (1) 
 
Where: 
PEFC,j,y = Are the CO 2 emissions from fossil fuel combustion in process j during the year y 
(tCO2/yr) 
FCi,j,y = Is the quantity of fuel type I combusted in process during the year y (mass or volume 
unit/yr) 
COEFi,y = Is the CO 2 emission coefficient of fuel type in year y (tCO 2/mass or volume unit)  
i = Are the fuel types combusted in process j during the year y  
As per TOOL03, in order to determine the parameter COEF, option B is the preferred approach as 
the necessary data is available.  
COEFi,y = NCVi,y x EFCO2,i,y Equation (4) 
 
Where: 
COEFi,y  = Is the CO 2 emission coefficient of fuel type in year y (tCO 2/mass or volume unit)  
NCVi,y  = Is the weighted average net calorific value of the fuel type i in year y (GJ/mass or 
volume unit) 
EFCO2,i,y  = Is the CO 2 emission coefficient of fuel type in year y (tCO 2/mass or volume unit)  
i  = Are the fuel types combusted in process j during the year y  
The project has a diesel generator, 630 kVA Alimar Diesel generator. The fuel used in the project 
is commercial diesel fuel and the quantities are included in the project emission calculations.  
Project emissions from flaring  (PEflare,y) 
Methodological TOOL06 describes the calculation procedure to determine the project emissions 
from flaring the residual gas project emission, flare based on the flare efficiency (flare) and the 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
100 
mass flow of methane to the flare (F CH4,RG,m). The flare efficiency is determined based on 
monitored data or default values.  
The methodological TOOL shows a procedure form three steps to calculate those emissions.  
(a) STEP 1: Determination of the methane mass flow of the residual  gas; 
 
The residual gas is monitored continuously in normal conditions (Nm 3/h). As per TOOL08 v3.0, 
The mass flow of methane in the residual gas in (kg/m) is calculated  
𝐹𝑖,𝑡 = 𝑉𝑖,𝑡 𝑥 𝑣𝑖,𝑡 𝑥 𝜌𝑖,𝑡 Equation (5) 
Where: 
 
Fi,t  = Mass flow of greenhouse gas i in the gaseous stream in time interval t (kg gas/h)  
Vi,t  = Volumetric flow of the gaseous stream in time interval t on a dry basis (m³ dry gas/h)  
vi,t  = Volumetric fraction of greenhouse gas i in the gaseous stream in a time interval t (m³ 
gas i/m³ dry gas)  
ρi,t  = Density of greenhouse gas i in the gaseous stream in time interval t (kg gas i/m³ gas i  
 
Paragraph 23 of TOOL08 states that, Flow measurement on a dry basis is not doable for a wet 
gaseous stream. Therefore, it  is necessary to demonstrate that the gaseous stream is dry to use 
this option. There are  two ways to do this: 
(c) Measure the moisture content of the gaseous stream (C H2O,t,db,n) and demonstrate  that this 
is less or equal to 0.05 kg H 2O/m3 dry gas; or 
(d) Demonstrate that the temperature of the gaseous stream (T t) is less than 60ºC (333.15 K) 
at the flow measurement point.  
With:

methane/m³ gaseous stream)

Open flare is selected. η flare,m = 50% 
The operating temperature of conveco’s flare system is 850 – 1200 °C, and according to records

text_blocks.heading: 5.3 Leakage
text_blocks.text:
There is no leakage described for methodology ACM0001.  
Leakage emissions are associated with anaerobic digestion and the use of RDF/SB that is 
exported outside the project boundary. Leakage emissions are determined as follows  
(ACM0022): 
𝐿𝐸𝑦 = 𝐿𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀𝑃,𝑦 + 𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 + 𝐿𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 Equation (32) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝑦 = Leakage emissions in the year  y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝐶𝑂𝑀𝑃,𝑦 = Leakage emissions from composting or co -composting in year y 
(t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = Leakage emissions from anaerobic digester in year  y (t CO2e) 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
102 
𝐿𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB in year  y (t CO2e) 
 
Since there is no composting activity under project activity, Leakage emissions associated with 
composting (LECOMP,y) is considered zero”. LE AD,y and LERDF_SB,y are considered for the project 
activity. In the Project case LE Storage,y is considered zero as the storage lagoons are -aerated. 
Leakage emissions from anaerobic digestion (LE AD,y) 
As per TOOL14 v03.0, Leakage emissions associated with the anaerobic digester (𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦) depend 
on how the digestate is managed. They include emissions associated with storage and 
composting of the digestate and are determined as follows:  
𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦  =  𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦  + 𝐿𝐸𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑝,𝑦 Equation (5) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝐴𝐷,𝑦 = Leakage emissions from anaerobic digester in year  y (tCO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑝,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with composting digestate in year 
y (tCO2e) 
Determination of leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate (LE storage,y) 
This step applies in the case that the digestate is stored under the following anaerobic conditions: 
(a) In an un-aerated lagoon that has a depth of more than one meter; or  
(b) In a SWDS, including stockpiles that are considered a SWDS as per the definitions 
section. 
Storage of digestate under anaerobic conditions can cause CH 4 emissions due to further 
anaerobic digestion of the residual biodegradable organic matter. The procedure for determining 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 is distinguished for liquid digestate and solid digestate.  
Determining LEstorage,y for liquid digestate 
Where digestate is liquid, as per the definitions section, or where a liquid fraction of mechanically 
separated digestate is stored, then choose between Options 1 or 2 below to determine 𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦. 
Option 1: Procedure using monitored data  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = Q𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟ed,𝑦  x PCOD,𝑦 x B0 x MCF x GWPCH4 Equation (6) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
103 
𝑄𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑒𝑑,𝑦 = Amount of liquid digestate stored anaerobically in year y (m 3) 
𝑃𝐶𝑂𝐷,𝑦 = 
Average chemical oxygen demand (COD) of the liquid digestate in 
year y (tCOD / m 3) 
𝐵0 = 
Maximum methane producing capacity of the COD applied (tCH 4 / 
tCOD) 
𝑀𝐶𝐹 = Methane conversion factor (fraction)  
𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (tCO2 / tCH4) 
 
Option 2: Procedure using a default value  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = Fww,CH4,default  x QCH4,𝑦 x GWPCH4 Equation (7) 
Where: 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
Fww,CH4,default = 
Default factor representing the remaining methane production 
capacity of liquid digestate (fraction)  
QCH4,𝑦  = Quantity of methane produced in the digester in year y (tCH 4) 
𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (tCO2 / tCH4) 
 
Determining LE storage,y for solid digestate 
Where solid digestate is disposed in a SWDS or a stockpile that can be considered a SWDS, as 
per the definition section, then project participants may choose between Option 1 or Option 2 to 
determine 𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦. 
Option 1: Procedure using monitored data  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 is determined using the methodological tool “Emissions from solid waste disposal 
sites”. In this case, 𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 corresponds to the parameter 𝐿𝐸𝐶𝐻4,𝑆𝑊𝐷𝑆,𝑦 in the tool and j 
represents the digestate that is disposed at a SWDS.  
Option 2: Procedure using default values  
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 is determined as follows: 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = FSD,CH4,default  x QCH4,𝑦 x GWPCH4 Equation (8) 
 
Where: 
 
 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
104 
𝐿𝐸𝑠𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒,𝑦 = 
Leakage emissions associated with storage of digestate in year y 
(tCO2e) 
Fww,CH4,default = 
Default factor representing the remaining methane production 
capacity of liquid digestate (fraction)  
QCH4,𝑦  = Quantity of methane produced in the digester in year y (tCH 4) 
𝐺𝑊𝑃𝐶𝐻4 = Global warming potential of CH 4 (tCO2 / tCH4) 
 
Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB (LE RDF_SB,y) 
According to methodology ACM0022 v03.0 , Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB are 
accounted for the organic waste by -products of the treatment process (not by -products from the 
RDF/SB combustor), which may be composted or disposed of in a SWDS, and the end -use of 
RDF/SB that is exported off -site, as follows: 
𝐿𝐸𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = 𝐿𝐸𝐸𝑁𝐷𝑈𝑆𝐸_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 + 𝐿𝐸𝑆𝑊𝐷𝑆,𝑊𝐵𝑃_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 Equation (33) 
 
Where: 
LERDF_SB,y  =Leakage emissions associated with RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2e) 
LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y  =Leakage emissions associated with disposing of waste by products 
associated with RDF/SB production in a SWDS in year y (t CO 2e) 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   =Leakage emissions associated with the end -use of RDF/SB exported 
outside the project boundary in year y (t CO 2e) 
Leakage emissions from disposal of waste by -products from RDF/SB production in a SWDS 
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y is determined using “TOOL04: Emissions 
from solid waste disposal sites”. In the tool, x begins with the start of the CDM project activity and 
extends to the end of year y (e.g. emissions are calculated using Application B in the tool and waste 
disposed from the start of the first crediting period shall be considered). And Wj,x in the tool is the 
amount of organic waste contained in the waste by-products from the production of RDF/SB in year 
y (e.g. it do es not include waste by -products that are composted instead of being disposed to a 
SWDS in the project activity or waste by-products from the combustion of RDF/SB). 
RDF production is solely based on using certain fraction of municipal solid waste that is 
mechanically separated and mechanically processed on wet basis. Within mechanical separation 
processes, 2D wastes separated after ballistic separation recyclable materials are separated. 
Remaining material is converted into RDF and all of it considered and expected to be sold to the 
end-user. Output material contains organic fraction as well inorganic fraction (such as plastics, 
glass etc.). 
Based on this situation, no amount of by-product or trash is expected to be disposed of. Therefore  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
105 
(LESWDS,WBP_RDF_SB,y) is considered zero. 
 
Leakage emissions associated with end use of RDF/SB exported outside the project boundary 
(LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y ) 
According to ACM0022 methodology, the potential leakage emissions associated with the use of 
the RDF/SB that is exported outside the project boundary are that it may be combusted or 
decompose anaerobically has to be accounted for. Emissions are therefore calculated allowing for 
the situation that RDF/SB exported in year y may have three different end uses: 
 
• End use 1: documented evidence is provided that the RDF/SB exported off-site is used as 
raw material in fertilizer, ceramic manufacture or as a fuel that is combusted in a CDM 
project activity. In this case, no leakage emissions are estimated; 
• End use 2: documented evidence is provided that the RDF/SB exported off-site is 
combusted or used as a raw material in furniture: In this case, the RDF/SB is considered 
to be combusted and LEENDUSE,RDF_SB,y shall be calculated, according to procedure below; 
𝐿𝐸𝐸𝑁𝐷𝑈𝑆𝐸_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦 = 𝑄𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝐶𝑂𝑀,𝑦  𝑥 𝑁𝐶𝑉𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦  𝑥 𝐸𝐹𝐶𝑂2_𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦  Equation (34) 
Where: 
 
LEENDUSE_RDF_SB,y   = Leakage emissions of CO 2 from off-site combustion of RDF/SB in year 
y (t CO2) 
QRDF_SB,COM,y   = Quantity of RDF/SB exported off -site with potential to be combusted 
in year y (t) 
EFCO2_RDF_SB,y  =CO2 emissions factor for RDF/SB in year y (t CO 2/GJ) 
NCVRDF_SB,y  = Net calorific value of RDF/SB in year y (GJ/t)  
• Leakage emissions from anaerobic decay of RDF/SB are accounted by adjusting the 
quantity of organic waste used to produce RDF/SB (WRDF_SB,j,x) in the calculation of 
baseline emissions from solid waste disposal sites, as follows: 
𝑊𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑗,𝑥,𝑎𝑑𝑗 = 𝑄𝑒𝑥𝑝𝑜𝑟𝑡,𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦
𝑄𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑦
× 𝑊𝑅𝐷𝐹_𝑆𝐵,𝑗,𝑥 Equation (35) 
Where: 
 
WRDF_SB,j,x,adj   =Amount of solid waste type j prevented from disposal in the SWDS by 
using the waste to produce RDF/SB in the year x, adjusted by the proportion of RDF/SB that is 
disposed of in a SWDS (t)  
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
106 
Qexport,RDF_SB,y  = Amount of RDF/SB exported offsite with potential to decay 
anaerobically in year y (t)  
QRDF_SB,y  = Amount of RDF/SB produced by the project activity in year y (t)  
WRDF_SB,j,x  =Amount of solid waste type j prevented from disposal in the SWDS by 
using the waste to produce RDF/SB in the year x (t)  
Current activity is the utilization of RDF under global carbon council projects Cine Biomass Power 
Plant project (ID S00508)44 and Ezine Biomass Power Plant Project (ID S01232) 45. Therefore, no 
leak emission from RDF production is accounted for.

text_blocks.heading: 5.4 Estimated Net GHG Emission Reductions and Removals
text_blocks.text:
To calculate the emission reductions the project participant shall apply the following equation:  
𝐸𝑅𝑦 = 𝐵𝐸𝑦 − 𝑃𝐸𝑦 − 𝐿𝐸𝑦 (Equation 36) 
Where: 
𝐸𝑅𝑦 = Emissions reductions in year y (t CO2e) 
𝐵𝐸𝑦 = Baseline emissions in year y (t CO2e) 
𝑃𝐸𝑦 = Project emissions in the year y (t CO2e) 
𝐿𝐸𝑦 = Leakage emissions in year y (t CO2e)

Data unit Dimensionless 
Description Fraction of methane that would be oxidized in the top layer of the

Source of data First order decay model from the TOOL 04 “Emissions from solid 
waste disposal sites” (Version 08.1).  
Value applied 0.1 
Justification of choice of 
data or description of 
measurement methods 
and procedures applied  
OXtop-layer is the fraction of the methane in the LFG that would 
oxidize in the top layer of the SWDS in the absence of the project 
activity. 
Under the project activity, this effect is reduced as a part of the 
LFG is captured and does not pass through the top layer of the 
SWDS. This oxidation effect is also accounted for in the 
methodological tool “Emissions from solid waste disposal sites”.  
 Purpose of Data The oxidation factor shall be included in the calculation of baseline 
emissions whereas the effect of oxidation is, as a conservative 
assumption, neglected under the project activity.  
Comments The oxidation factor represents the proportion of methane that is 
oxidized to CO 2 This should be distinguished from the methane 
correction factor (MCF) which is to account for the situation that

115 
Data unit kg/m3 (TOOL06) t/m3 (TOOL08 & TOOL14) 
Description Density of methane gas at reference conditions 
Source of data TOOL06 “Project emissions from flaring”, TOOL08 “Tool to 
determine the mass flow of a greenhouse gas in a gaseous stream” 
and TOOL14 “Project and leakage emissions from anaerobic 
digesters” 
Value applied 0.716 kg/m3 (TOOL 06) and 0.000670 t/m3 (TOOL14) 
Justification of choice of 
data or description of 
measurement methods 
and procedures applied  
Under project activity, Biogas flow measurements are converted 
Nm3 by the flow meter51 (corrected volume flow, page 9) which uses 
data from connected to the temperature and pressure sensor. 
Thus, measured values are automatically converted to normal 
conditions (0 °C and 101,325 Pa). According to methane density 
document of CDM 52, 0.716 kg/m 3 is the density at normal 
conditions. 
Purpose of Data Determination of the methane mass flow of the biogas for the 
purpose of calculating baseline and project emissions 
Comments 0.716 kg/m 3 value is used for calculating project emissions from 
flaring (TOOL06) and baseline emissions from methane avoidance 
(TOOL08) while 0.000670 t/m 3 value is used for project emission 
calculation from anaerobic digesters (TOOL14)). The reason for the 
difference is T OOL06 references 0 °C standard conditions while 
TOOL14 reference to 20 °C normal conditions.

Description Universal ideal gases constant  
Source of data TOOL08 “Tool to determine the mass flow of a greenhouse gas in 
a gaseous stream”

and procedures applied

Source of data  Data is taken from Turkish Electricity Transmission Corporation 
(TEIAŞ)55 in accordance with Methodological Tool: Baseline, 
project and/or leakage emissions from electricity consumption 
and monitoring of electricity generation, Version 3.0 .  
Republic of Türkiye has a single national grid  and the official 
energy dataset (Annual development of electricity generation - 
consumption and losses in turkey) provides transmission and 
distribution losses  and a combined loss percentage. Both the 
project and baseline electricity consumption are sourced from the 
same transmission and distribution system.  Transmission and 
distribution losses are intrinsic to the physical infrastructure and 
operational conditions of the grid. The grid operator does not 
distinguish between separate user categories, thus TDL values are 
identical for all connected users .

Electricity meter is subject to regular (in accordance with 
stipulation of the meter supplier) maintenance and testing by 
regulation to ensure accuracy and TEİAŞ (Transmission System 
Operator) is responsible of calibrating these meters, which is a 
government-owned corporation. The readings are double checked 
by the electricity distribution company. The calibration of meter, 
including the frequenc y of calibration, will be done in accordance 
with national standards or requirements set by the meter supplier 
or requirements set by the grid operators. The accuracy class of 
the meter is in accordance with the stipulation of the meter 
supplier and/or as per the requirements set by the grid operators 
or national requirements.   
Monitored amounts can be tracked by official website TEİAŞ via 
following link: 
https://seffaflik.epias.com.tr/electricity/electricity -
generation/ex-post-generation/injection -quantity 
 Joint Project Description & Monitoring Report: VCS Version 4. 2 
124 
Electricity meters calibration interval is 10 years 56. 
Purpose of data  Calculation of baseline emissions  
Calculation method  Net electricity generation values can be obtained from meter 
readings by PO and the official settlement records. It has been 
observed that net electricity generation value is more 
conservative than the difference of Gross electricity generation 
and Electri city consumption. Therefore, values from the 
settlement records are used due to them being more conservative 
and accurate. UEVM (Settlement record net electricity supply) 
value from settlement records is used as gross electricity 
generation and UEÇM (Corre ction factor) value from settlement 
records. Invoicing is also based on settlement records.  
Comments  This parameter is required for calculating baseline emissions 
associated with electricity generation (BE EC,y) using the 
methodological tool “Baseline, project and/or leakage emissions 
from electricity consumption and monitoring of electricity 
generation”

For each equipment unit j using the LFG monitor that the plant is 
operating in hour h by the monitoring any one or more of the 
following three parameters:  
Temperature. Determine the location for temperature 
measurements and minimum operational temperature based on 
manufacturer’s specifications of the burning equipment. 
Document and justify the location and minimum threshold in the 
PDD; 
Flame. Flame detection system is used to ensure that the 
equipment is in operation;  
Products generated. Monitor the generation  
Opj,h = 0 when: 
One of more temperature measurements are missing below the 
minimum threshold in hour h (instantaneous measurements are 
made at least every minute);  
Flame is not detected continuously in hour h (instantaneous 
measurements are made at least every minute)  
Otherwise, O pj,h = 1

QA/QC procedures to be 
applied According to the document “CDM validation and verification 
standard for project activities” Version 03.0, paragraph 366. (a); 
When the calibration period is delayed, following procedure has 
to be applied: 
a) Applying the maximum permissible error of 
the instrument to the measured values taken 
during the period between the scheduled 
date of calibration and the actual date of 
calibration, if the results of the delayed 
calibration do not show any errors in the 
measuring equipment, or if the error is 
smaller than the maximum permissible error  
b) Applying the error identified in the delayed 
calibration test, if the error is beyond the 
maximum permissible error of the measuring 
equipment. 
According to device technical specification 59, maximum 
permissible error rate is 0.075% 60. When calibration is delayed, 
0.075% device accuracy rate will be subtracted from methane 
content reading.
